IA  No.  178357/2026  (Application  for  permission)  and  IA  No.
178214/2026 – (Application for intervention/impleadment)
1. Issue notice.  
2. List along with W.P. (Civil) No. 986/2025 on 22.07.2026.
3. Meanwhile, the applicant(s) shall be allowed to continue in
service till the age of 61 years, provided they have not already
been released from the service.
4. Having regard to the fact that this issue is likely to arise
in all the States, let notice be issued to all the High Courts,
States, Union Territories and the Union of India.

CORRECTED
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO. 3273        OF 2026
(@ SLP(CRL.) No.13007  OF 2026)
(@ Diary No(s). 57/2026)
SINDH RAJKUMAR ALIAS 
BACHCHA & ANR.                APPELLANT(S)
                     VERSUS
THE STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH        RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Delay condoned. 
2. Leave granted.
3. We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the appellants and the learned counsel appearing
for the respondent. 
4. The present case arises out of an occurrence
alleged  to  have  taken  place  on  29.08.1998
pursuant to which an FIR was registered against
the accused persons for the offence punishable
under Section 395 of the Indian Penal Code,
1860. 
5. The case of the prosecution is that the accused
persons entered the shop of the complainant and
committed dacoity by looting cash and certain
1

--- Page 46 ---
articles. The Trial Court, after considering the
evidence available on record, was pleased to
convict the appellants and sentenced them to
undergo rigorous imprisonment for a period of
ten years. On appeal, the High Court modified
the sentence to seven years. Unfortunately, the
learned counsel appearing for the appellants,
did not appear before the High Court. However,
the High Court, by taking note of the fact that
the Vakalatnama was on the record, proceeded to
decide the appeal on merits. 
6. Learned counsel appearing for the appellants
submitted that the appellants ought to have
given an opportunity to put forth their case on
merits.  The  delay,  though  substantial,  was
occasioned by the fact that they were unaware of
the disposal of the appeal.  It was further
submitted that the independent witnesses had
turned hostile. 
7. Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  State
submitted that the eye-witnesses examined by the
prosecution had deposed in favour of the charge
framed against the appellants. Both the Trial
Court and the High Court accepted the testimony
2

--- Page 47 ---
of  the  eye-witnesses  while  rendering  the
conviction.  Since there is no perversity in the
findings of the Courts below, there is no need
to interfere with the impugned judgment. On the
issue  of  the  non-appearance  of  the  counsel
before  the  High  Court,  the  learned  counsel
appearing for the State submitted that it is not
a case where the appellants had not been served.
On contrary, they had been duly served, and it
was only their counsel who failed to appear. 
8. Upon hearing the learned counsel appearing for
the appellants and the learned counsel appearing
for the respondent-State, we do not find any
ground  to  interfere  with  the  conviction
rendered. It is true that the appellants were
appearing before the Trial Court in compliance
with  the  conditions  imposed  while  granting
suspension  of  sentence.  From  this,  one  can
reasonably infer that they may not have been
aware of the disposal of the appeal. However,
the  High  Court  has  rightly  considered  the
relevant materials available on record while
confirming the conviction. 
9. That leaves us to the question of sentence.
3

--- Page 48 ---
The occurrence was of the year 1998. Now, nearly
28 years have lapsed. One of the appellants has
already  undergone  more  than  one  year  of
incarceration, while the other has undergone
more than two years of incarceration.
10. Taking note of the mitigating circumstances,
including the motive that led to the occurrence,
we are inclined to modify the sentence to the
period already undergone. 
11. Accordingly,  the  sentence  imposed  stands
modified to the period already undergone. 
12. The appeal stands allowed in part, accordingly. 
13. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.   
    
  ……………………………………………………………………J.
  [M.M. SUNDRESH]
       ………………………………………………………………………J.
     [PRASANNA B. VARALE]
 NEW DELHI;
 16th JULY, 2026
4

--- Page 49 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO.                   OF 2026
(@ SLP(CRL.) No.            OF 2026)
(@ Diary No(s). 57/2026)
SINDH RAJKUMAR ALIAS 
BACHCHA & ANR.                APPELLANT(S)
                     VERSUS
THE STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH        RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
14. Delay condoned. 
15. Leave granted.
16. We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the appellants and the learned counsel appearing
for the respondent. 
17. The present case arises out of an occurrence
alleged  to  have  taken  place  on  29.08.1998
pursuant to which an FIR was registered against
the accused persons for the offence punishable
under Section 395 of the Indian Penal Code,
1860. 
18. The case of the prosecution is that the accused
persons entered the shop of the complainant and
committed dacoity by looting cash and certain
5

--- Page 50 ---
articles. The Trial Court, after considering the
evidence available on record, was pleased to
convict the appellants and sentenced them to
undergo rigorous imprisonment for a period of
ten years. On appeal, the High Court modified
the sentence to seven years. Unfortunately, the
learned counsel appearing for the appellants,
did not appear before the High Court. However,
the High Court, by taking note of the fact that
the Vakalatnama was on the record, proceeded to
decide the appeal on merits. 
19. Learned counsel appearing for the appellants
submitted that the appellants ought to have
given an opportunity to put forth their case on
merits.  The  delay,  though  substantial,  was
occasioned by the fact that they were unaware of
the disposal of the appeal.  It was further
submitted that the independent witnesses had
turned hostile. 
20. Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  State
submitted that the eye-witnesses examined by the
prosecution had deposed in favour of the charge
framed against the appellants. Both the Trial
Court and the High Court accepted the testimony
6

--- Page 51 ---
of  the  eye-witnesses  while  rendering  the
conviction.  Since there is no perversity in the
findings of the Courts below, there is no need
to interfere with the impugned judgment. On the
issue  of  the  non-appearance  of  the  counsel
before  the  High  Court,  the  learned  counsel
appearing for the State submitted that it is not
a case where the appellants had not been served.
On contrary, they had been duly served, and it
was only their counsel who failed to appear. 
21. Upon hearing the learned counsel appearing for
the appellants and the learned counsel appearing
for the respondent-State, we do not find any
ground  to  interfere  with  the  conviction
rendered. It is true that the appellants were
appearing before the Trial Court in compliance
with  the  conditions  imposed  while  granting
suspension  of  sentence.  From  this,  one  can
reasonably infer that they may not have been
aware of the disposal of the appeal. However,
the  High  Court  has  rightly  considered  the
relevant materials available on record while
confirming the conviction. 
22. That leaves us to the question of sentence.
7

--- Page 52 ---
The occurrence was of the year 1998. Now, nearly
28 years have lapsed. One of the appellants has
already  undergone  more  than  one  year  of
incarceration, while the other has undergone
more than two years of incarceration.
23. Taking note of the mitigating circumstances,
including the motive that led to the occurrence,
we are inclined to modify the sentence to the
period already undergone. 
24. Accordingly,  the  sentence  imposed  stands
modified to the period already undergone. The
appellants shall be released forthwith, unless
their custody is required in any other case.
25. The appeal stands allowed in part, accordingly. 
26. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

--- Page 68 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
CIVIL APPEAL NO.               OF 2026
(@ D. NO. 12620/2026)
M/S ALPHA RADIOS                Appellant(s)
VERSUS
NATIONAL INSURANCE COMPANY LIMITED         Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The present Appeal calls in question the correctness of the final order
dated 23.12.2025 passed by the  National Consumer Disputes Redressal
Commission [for short “NCDRC”] in Complaint Case No. 803 of 2018.
By the said order, the NCDRC partly allowed the complaint and directed
the respondent to pay the appellant a sum of Rs.1,05,03,268/- along with
interest @ 6 per cent.  Aggrieved the appellant is in appeal.
3. At the outset, we must point out that we have by a detailed order of
13.07.2026 disposed of the appeal of M/s Nikon Systems Private Limited
1           

--- Page 69 ---
vs. National Insurance Company Limited (Civil Appeal No.8818 of 2026).
Except for the differences pointed out hereinbelow, the premises in which
the fire occurred as well the surveyors who were involved in the process
are all common.  The differences are in the date of the policy, the amount
claimed, date of repudiation and assessment of loss.
4. In  this  appeal  as  well  as  in  the  appeal  of  Nikon  (supra),  the
complainant was aggrieved by the amount awarded by the NCDRC. In
Nikon (supra), there  was also cross-appeal by the  Insurance Company-
Opposite  party.   In  the  present  case,  there  is  no  cross  appeal,  but  a
grievance has been raised by the opposite party that none of its contents
have been discussed.  The order of this Court dated 13.07.2026 in Nikon
(supra) ought to be read alongside this order.
5. The facts, insofar as this appeal is concerned, are that the appellant
filed before the NCDRC a complaint seeking the following reliefs : -
“In view of the foregoing facts and submissions, the Complainant most
humbly prays that this Hon'ble Commission may be pleased to:-
(a) allow the Claim and direct the Opposite Party to pay a sum of Rs.
3,95,69,987/-  (Rupees  Three  Crore  Ninety-Five  Lakh  Sixty  Nine
Thousand Nine Hundred and Eighty Seven Only) as compensation for
deficiency of service being loss suffered by the Complainant due to fire;
(b) direct the Opposite Party to pay Rs.33,82,419/- (Rupees Thirty Three
Lakh  Eighty  Two  Thousand  Four  Hundred  and  Nineteen  Only)  on
account of the Loss suffered and rent paid for lease of the premises from
5.12.2014 to 31.8.2015 @ Rs.1,40,000/- per month;
2           

--- Page 70 ---
(c) direct the Opposite Party to pay Rs.4,40,177/- (Rupees Four Lakh
Forty Thousand One Hundred and Seventy Seven Only) on account of
the Loss suffered and salaries paid by the Complainant to three security
guards (8 hours each) from the date of fire i.e. 5.12.2014 to the date of
lifting of damage material/debris/burnt malba residue i.e. 15.1.2017 @
Rs. 11,000/- per month
(d)  direct  the  Opposite  Party  to  pay  to  the  Complainant  a  sum  of
Rs.25,645/-  (Rupees  Twenty  Five  Thousand  Six  Hundred  and  Forty
Five)  on  account  of  expenses  incurred  for  disposal  of  damage
material/debris/burnt malba residue;
(e) direct the Opposite Party to pay to the Complainant Rs.25,00,000/-
(Rupees Twenty Five Lakh Only) towards harassment and mental agony
suffered by the Complainant and its Partners;
(f) direct the Opposite Party to pay to the Complainant Rs. 10,00,000/-
(Rupees  Ten  lakhs  only)  towards  the  expenses  incurred  for  hiring
various professionals, consultants, Advocates etc.
(g)  direct  the  Opposite  Party  to  pay  to  the  Complainant  interest  on
claims (a) to (f) at the rate of 18% per annum from the date of the loss
i.e. 5.12.2014 till the date of the payment.
(h) pass such other order or orders as this Hon'ble Commission may
deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the present case.”
6. The facts are that the Complainant took a Standard Fire and Special
Peril  Insurance  Policy  bearing  No.  350200/11/13/3300000518  from
13.03.2014 to 12.03.2015 to cover the risk of the goods belonging to the
Complainant  and  lying  at  Rangpuri  godown.   A  fire  broke  out  on
05.12.2014 between 3.00 PM and 3.15 PM where the goods/stocks of the
Complainant Company and Nikon (supra) were  kept.  The Firefighting
operations went on for a long period with 32 fire tenders being deployed.
3           

--- Page 71 ---
7. Oral  intimation  was  given  to  the  Opposite  Party  and  a  written
communication was sent on 06.12.2014.  Like in the case of Nikon (supra),
M/s  S.  Soni  &  Co.,  were  appointed  as  Surveyors,  who  prepared  a
preliminary report on 08.12.2014.  The Complainant alleges that the report
has not been given to them in spite of requests.  Similarly, like in Nikon
(supra), M/s J.C. Gupta & Co.  was appointed as Second Surveyor and
thereafter, one C.A. Dilip Kumar Saha was appointed and together, M/s
J.C.  Gupta  &  Co.  and  C.A.  Dilip  Kumar  were  constituted  as  Joint
Surveyors.
8. Inspection was carried on and lot of back and forth happened between
the Complainant and the Surveyors.  In the present case also, J. Basheer
was appointed as Investigator as in Nikon (supra).
 
9. We are not delving into the correspondence in detail, considering the
nature  of  the  order  we  propose  to  make  in  this  case  also.   The  Joint
Surveyors prepared their report dated 31.01.2017. The Joint Surveyors, in
their  report  of  31.01.2017,  assessed  the  loss  at  Rs.1,05,03,268/-  and
recommended indemnification of the said loss.  However, they observed
that the underwriter was to take the final decision.  It also transpires that the
adjusted loss of Rs. 1,05,03,268/- was revised by the Surveyors by a mail
of 18.03.2017 to  Rs. 91,84,382/-. There is a slight difference in this aspect
4           

--- Page 72 ---
with  the  case  of Nikon(supra).   In Nikon (supra),  after arriving at the
adjusted loss of Rs. 1,05,03,268/-, a  recommendation was made  not to
indemnify.  Be that as it may.
10. The appellant filed a claim for Rs.4,69,18,228/- before the Opposite
Party though in the final prayer a claim of Rs.3,95,69,987/- along with
certain claims under other heads was made.
11. Based on the above facts, several deficiencies were alleged on the part
of the Opposite Party in view of the total repudiation.  Many of them have
been tabulated in the order in Nikon (supra) and which are also mostly
common in the case of the present appellant.
12. A detailed reply was filed by the Opposite Party stating that the whole
claim was repudiated by them by their own letter of 04.05.2018 for the
reasons set out in the letter.  The reply also discussed the report of the Joint
Surveyor dated 31.01.2017 and the final analysis made by them.  It also
sets out that the quantity claimed were based on opening balance/stocks as
on 01.04.2017 which itself was not based on any verifiable record.  The
total  Delhi  stock,  according  to  the  Surveyor,  was  not  verifiable  in  the
absence  of  any  supporting/basic  records.   The  reply  set  out  that  all
necessary details were not furnished and the principle of uberrima fide was
breached by the Complainant.  Deficiency in service was denied and it was
5           

--- Page 73 ---
sought that the complaint be dismissed.
13. The  NCDRC  has  by  a  summary  order  disposed  of  the  original
complaint.  The operative part of the order is extracted hereinbelow: - 
“5.   Heard  learned  counsels  for  both  the  parties in  detail  and
carefully perused the records. The terms of contract entered into
between the parties, the date of fire incident on 05.12.2014 and the
belated  claim  that  was  the  Appellant  filed  on  12.04.2016  are
undisputed.  While OP alleged fraud against the Appellant, the loss
that occasioned on 05.12.2014 is not in dispute. The matter was
immediately  reported  by  the  Appellant  and  OP appointed  the
surveyor. While the Appellant's claim for loss was Rs.3,95,69,987,
the total insured cover was Rs.4,00,00,000 and the net assessed
loss  determined  by  the  surveyor  was  Rs.  1,05,03,268/-.
Considering the very nature of the insurance contract providing
for  insurance  cover  for  stocks  at  multiple  locations  and  the
commercial  activity  involved,  there  were  delays  in
communications and lapses in providing records and details. In
our considered view, however, these delays/lapses do not reflect
indulging in fraudulent actions by the Appellant.
6.   After  careful  consideration  of  the  entire  facts  and
circumstances of the case, including the submissions made by the
learned  counsels  for  the  both  the  parties,  we  consider  it
appropriate to partly allow the complaint directing the Respondent
to  pay  the  Appellant  Rs.  1,05,03,268/-,  as  determined  by  the
surveyor vide Final Joint Survey Report dated 31.01.2017, along
with interest @ 6% per annum from the date commencing from
six months after the date of filing the claim till the date of final
payment. This payment shall be made within three months from
today. In the event of delay, the interest payable for the delayed
period shall be @ 9% per annum.”
14. We have heard Mr. Gagan Gupta, learned senior counsel and Ms.
Minakshi Midha, learned counsel for the Opposite Party.  Mr. Gagan Gupta,
learned senior counsel contended that the order of the NCDRC is a non-
speaking order and without adducing any reasons as to how the NCDC
6           

--- Page 74 ---
reached the conclusion of the quantum of compensation at Rs.1,05,03,268/-
when the total loss claimed was Rs.3,95,69,987/-.
15. Attention of the learned senior counsel was drawn to para 2 of the
impugned order which contains the following statement: -
“2. The learned counsel…… He further states that, at this stage, the
Complainant would be satisfied if the claim is settled by the OP as
determined by the surveyor.”
16. Learned senior counsel, in response, submitted that their claim was
based upon the report of the first Surveyor Ms. Soni & Co.  It is not clear
from the record what was the amount determined by M/s Soni & Co.  
17. However, be that as it may, the impugned order also carries no reasons
whatsoever.  Learned counsel for the Insurance Company also stated that
they will have no objection if the matter is remanded since none of their
contentions about total repudiation of the policy has been discussed. 
18. We  have  considered  the  submissions  of  the  learned  counsels  and
perused the records. We find that the impugned order is totally bereft of
reasons. [See Kranti Associates Private Limited  and Another vs. Masood
Ahmed Khan and Others 1] The proceeding was an original proceeding
before the NCDRC. On multiple factual issues, parties were at loggerheads.
No doubt, the proceedings were before the NCDRC and not before the Civil
1(2010) 9 SCC 496
7           

--- Page 75 ---
Court.  However,  there  ought  to  be  an  analysis  al  beit briefly  of  the
contentions and recording of findings. It is also not clear how the NCDRC
assumed that the counsel was conceding to receive the without prejudice
amount arrived at by the joint Surveyors. Para 2 which we have extracted
above, does not establish the fact that it was the without prejudice amount
of Rs. 1,087,25,027/- that the counsel was alluding to.
19. In a classic passage that repays study, this Court in Union of India vs.
Mohal Lal Capoor and Others2 explained the concept of Reasons thus:
“28. …. Reasons are the links between the materials on which certain
conclusions are based and the actual conclusions.  They disclose how the
mind is applied to the subject matter for a decision whether it is purely
administrative or quasi-judicial.  They should reveal a rational nexus
between the facts considered and the conclusions reached.  Only in this
way can opinions or decisions recorded be shown to be manifestly just
and reasonable…..”
20. The manner of disposal of the original complaint has been totally
unsatisfactory. As the pleadings and contentions of parties which have been
set out hereinabove establish, while the complainant sought for award of
Rs. 4,95,69,987/-, the Opposite Party was praying for total repudiation.
21. For  the  reasons  stated  above,  we  allow  this  appeal,  set  aside  the
impugned order and remand the matter back to the NCDRC for a fresh
2 (1973) 2 SCC 836 at 854
8           

--- Page 76 ---
adjudication  in  accordance  with  law,  uninfluenced  by  any  of  the
observations made herein.  The appeal is allowed.  The impugned order
dated 23.12.2025 in Consumer Complaint No. 803 of 2018 is restored to the
file of the NCDRC for de novo adjudication in accordance with law.  No
order as to costs. 
……………………………………, J.
[ K. V . VISWANATHAN ]  
 ..…………...……………………., J.
         [ ARUN PALLI ]   
New Delhi;
July 16,  2026.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The  larger  issue  that  falls  for  consideration  in  these
proceedings revolves around certain benefits/facilities to be made
admissible to the retired Chief Justices and Judges of the High
Courts. It is a matter of record that basic facilities, like
domestic  help,  driver,  telephone,  medical  reimbursement,  and
transit stay in Government Guest Houses/Circuit Houses, accorded to
them vary from State to State. The lack of uniformity has prompted
the  Association  of  Retired  Judges  to  firstly  approach  the
authorities, and thereafter to invoke the jurisdiction of this
Court.
2. It appears to us that the Union of India is also a vital
stakeholder in these proceedings, especially when we expect that
the financial burden ought to be shared by the Union of India and
the States in a defined, proportionate manner. There can indeed be
no doubt that these facilities must be ensured on uniform basis,
and there is no reason whatsoever for such amenities being at
variance from State to State.
3. Keeping these factors in mind, we have impressed upon learned
8

--- Page 96 ---
Solicitor General of India to persuade the Union of India for
constitution of a Committee to lay down uniform guidelines on this
matter. Let such a Committee be constituted within two weeks. There
shall also be an advance request to the Committee to submit its
recommendations to the Union of India as also to place on record
before this Court within three months from the date of its
constitution.  
4. Post this matter on 03.11.2026.  
(NITIN TALREJA)                            (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR

--- Page 101 ---
Mr. Avijit Mani Tripathi, AOR
Mr. T.K. Nayak, Adv.
Mr. Divyakant Lahoti, AOR
Ms. Madhur Jhavar, Adv.
Mr. Shivendra Dwivedi, AAG
Mr. Vivek Singh, AOR

Ms. Sabarni Som, Adv.
Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, AOR
Ms. Sonakshi Sinha, Adv.
Mr. Anup K. Rattan, AG
Mr. Vaibhav Srivastava, AAG
Mr. Puneet Rajia, AAG
Ms. Sugandha Anand, AOR
Mr. Kamal Balkrishna Trivedi, AG
Mr. Swapneel Sejpal, Adv.
Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, AOR
Ms. Sonakshi Sinha, Adv.                        
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. On oral prayer of the learned ASG, respondent No.1 is deleted
from the array of parties. Respondent No. 2 stands renamed as
‘Union of India, through Principal Secretary, Ministry of Law and
Justice, 4th Floor, A-Wing, Shastri Bhawan, New Delhi-110001’.
2. I.A. No.197568/2026 (application for impleadment) is allowed.
The applicants are permitted to be impleaded as party petitioners.
3. In deference to our order dated 19.06.2026, learned Attorney
General for India along with all the States/Union Territories
through their Advocates General and Standing Counsel have entered
appearance.
5

--- Page 102 ---
4. Multiple  issues  of  paramount  public  interest  have  been
deliberated in the course of the hearing.
5. One of the prayers made through the instant petition is the
creation and operationalisation of a corpus of funds for financial
assistance to the young advocates in the initial years of their
practice. The Learned Attorney General for India has volunteered to
convene a meeting of all the Advocates General and standing counsel
for States and Union Territories to discuss the modalities for
creation of the said corpus. We have no reason to doubt that all
the States will come forward and volunteer to provide a mechanism
through which substantial contribution to the proposed corpus can
be made. We, therefore, request the learned Attorney General for
India to convene the meeting at the earliest, at his convenience,
and apprise this Court of its outcome.
6. The other principal prayer concerns the absence of adequate
washroom and sanitation facilities for women advocates in courts
across the country.
7. Access to basic health necessities is an essential part of the
right to life, a principle which has, time and again, been the
foundation of judicial pronouncements by this Court. That being so,
it is indisputable that access to a washroom amounts to an
inalienable and universal fundamental right traceable to Article 21
of the Constitution, especially the right to live a healthy and
dignified life.
8. The claim for ensuring such right, furthermore, assumes a
deeper shade when it comes to the circumstances highlighted in the
instant  petition,  namely,  the  pitiful  condition  of  washrooms
6

--- Page 103 ---
available for women in public judicial buildings, such as High
Courts, District Courts, and Sub-Divisional Courts across India. It
has been pointed out that in some places, there is no washroom for
women whatsoever in the public areas of the court premises. In
other cases, although a room designated as “women’s washroom”
exists, the same is not adequately fitted, does not have running
water, or is kept in a sanitary condition. The women members of the
bar are, thereby, forced to walk long distances, across multiple
buildings, to find a usable washroom, if any available in the first
place.
9. That the circumstances are such, in our opinion, is a travesty
of justice. The lack of sanitary facilities for women lawyers is
prima facie discriminatory and violative of Articles 14 and 15.
Moreover, when many young female lawyers are looking to learn and
grow in the profession, the absence of such basic human facilities
creates an insurmountable hurdle for their entry and continuation
in the profession, raising graver concerns regarding the freedom to
practice the profession under Article 19(1)(g). In our considered
opinion, no institutional efforts to improve participation of women
lawyers in the profession, especially in the Courts, will bear any
fruit until it is ensured that women can work in courtrooms in a
healthy and dignified manner. There is no gainsaying that such duty
of the State is constitutionally acknowledged under Article 42.
10. With these tenets in mind, we have requested all the Advocates
General to obtain a fact-finding report with respect to the
existence and state of such infrastructure for lady members of the
bar in High Courts, District Courts, as well as Taluka Courts. They
7

--- Page 104 ---
shall do the needful within two weeks.
11. Thereafter, a proposal shall be put up before the State
Government for allocation of special funds to construct washrooms
and ensure accompanying water and sanitation facilities. It shall
be the responsibility of the Public Works Departments in all the
States/Union Territories to commence the construction of washrooms
with requisite facilities, wherever the same is not available,
within four weeks from the date of submission of the reports by the
learned Advocates General to the State Government. 
12. We make it abundantly clear that non-availability of funds
will not be accepted as any level of justification for delay in
initiation of the work. This is especially so, given that the
denial of basic amenities, like washrooms, running water, and
sanitary conditions, has been consistently held by this Court to be
a failure of the right to a dignified life, which is, indeed, a
basic human right as well.
13. A status report by each State Government/Union Territory shall
be filed within a period of six weeks, highlighting the information
received as well as steps taken to construct the washrooms. 
14. This Court shall not entertain any request for leniency in the
time schedule prescribed hereinabove, and every State Government
shall be obligated to do the needful within the stipulated period.
15. List for compliance and further orders on 11.09.2026.
(NITIN TALREJA)                            (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR
8

--- Page 105 ---
REVISED

1

--- Page 106 ---
of justice, requested Ms. Visakhonuo Natso, (Email ID:

stayed. An endeavour shall be made to have all disputes
arising out of matrimonial discord, whether pending before
different forums or under contemplation, settled amicably.

complied with by the respondent-husband.

continue to assist the Court as such.

                   Ms. Tanya Sharma, Adv.
  
O  R  D  E  R
The  Primus  Resident  Condominium  Association  (hereinafter  
referred to as the ‘PRCA’) stands superseded with immediate effect  
and until further orders by this Court. The Commissioner of Police,  
Gurugram is directed to immediately take over the PRCA’s premises  

--- Page 114 ---
4
and  secure  all  its  records/documents.  The  PRCA  shall  be  
administered by the Deputy Commissioner, Gurugram. Needless to  
state, any resident of the building/complex shall not be put to any  
inconvenience by the said Officers. Likewise, no resident shall  
interfere in the performance of the exercise we direct.
2.  The Deputy Commissioner, Gurugram, shall forthwith get a  
detailed audit conducted apropos the functioning of the PRCA from  
the date of its inception till date. Let the audit be carried out  
within three days. 
3. The Deputy Commissioner, Gurugram, shall ensure that the PRCA  
is properly managed and bona fide grievances of all residents of  
the complex are addressed in accordance with law, behoving the  
manner of functioning of a Residents Welfare Association. 
4. We have heard arguments from all the parties  in extenso . 
However, on a basic factual aspect, the Court is unable to form a  
definitive opinion. The same relates primarily to whether the 24-
metre wide road built inside the area of the present developed site  
by DLF Home Developers Limited (hereinafter referred to as ‘DLF’)  
is totally free of any encroachment/encumbrance or whether there  
are structures which impede movement of traffic and the use of the  
said portion as a road. We are also not very sure as to whether the  
point at which the road ends and connects to the existing 60-metre  
wide road is fully open or is restricted by the construction of a  
gate, and if so, what is the width of such gate.
5. Accordingly, for being aware of the true picture on the  
ground, the parties agreed that in their presence at the site day  
after tomorrow i.e., on 23.07.2026 at 9 AM, the concerned area(s)  

--- Page 115 ---
5
be video-graphed and photographed.
6. Further, there is an issue as to whether all occupants have  
been given permanent electric connections and whether they have  
been connected to the common backup/generator system. The State  
Authorities shall also submit a report as to whether as per the  
requisition of DLF, the installed transformers are of adequate  
capacity to bear the load. Material shall be placed to show whether  
a fully functional/permanent arrangement to handle the sewage for  
the entire campus is in place.
7. For Paras 5 and 6 supra, the team shall comprise officers of  
the  Government  of  Haryana/concerned  Authorities;  the  
petitioners/their representatives; DLF representatives, and; Mr.  
Saurav Gupta, SP, CBI, who has given a report alongwith his team.  
Support in terms of required personnel be had from the Government  
of Haryana, which must include the Departments of Power and Public  
Health  Engineering.  The  Haryana  Fire  and  Emergency  Services  
officials shall also visit the site, undertake a review and submit  
a report as to whether all fire safety statutes/regulations/norms  
have been complied with,  in respect of the entire complex as also  
the individual flats/apartments/buildings. Costs of inspection etc. 
for the present exercise, be borne by the Government of Haryana.
8. Inspection to commence on 23.07.2026 from 9 AM and the team  
shall not be under any compulsion to conclude the same in one day  
and a spillover for an additional day i.e., 24.07.2026 is also  
permitted. Mr. Saurav Gupta, SP, CBI, may coordinate the inspection  
and allied logistics.
9. List on 28.07.2026 at 2.30 PM.

--- Page 116 ---
6
10.  Para 16 of our Order dated 25.02.2026 is yet operative, and by  
this Order, we do not conclude/foreclose any submissions by DLF/any  
other entity.
11. After the order was dictated, prayer was made by learned  
counsel for the PRCA to reconsider the Order for supersession of  
the PRCA. Having considered the prayer, the directions issued in  
Paras 1, 2 and 3 above are kept in abeyance, for the present.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                         (ANJALI PANWAR)
    COURT MASTER (SH)                           ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No.6410/2026
[Arising  out  of  impugned  final  judgment  and  order  dated

STATE OF M.P & ANR.                           Respondent(s)
IA No. 109400/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Vaibhav Srivastava, Adv.
                   Ms. Sugandha Anand, AOR
                   Mr. Chetan Anand Mohapatra, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Ms. Manisha T. Karia, Sr. A.A.G.

                   Mr. Pashupathi Nath Razdan, AOR
                   
                   
SLP(Crl.)  No.6410/2026                                           1

--- Page 118 ---
       UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The Petitioner sought extension of parole on medical
grounds, specifically for his treatment for Carcinoma
Prostate with Bone Metastasis. The Petitioner is now
suffering also from complications arising after surgery.
2. The Petitioner was convicted by Sessions Court Byabra,
District  Rajgarh,  MP  for  offences  punishable  under
Sections 147, 148, 342, 302 read with 149, and 323 read
with 140 of Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short “the IPC”)
vide  judgment  dated  19.12.2001  in  Sessions  Case  No.
12/2000 and has been inter alia imposed with a life
sentence.
3. The petitioner approached the High Court through a Writ
Petition  and  was  allowed  special  parole  between  the
period from 08.08.2025 to 10.12.2025 which was extended
from time to time on medical grounds. Madhya Pradesh High
Court in order dated 10.10.2025 in WP No.40084/2025 had
extended the parole for a period of 2 months with the
stipulation that no further extension or parole under any
circumstance shall be granted to the Petitioner.
4. The Petitioner is 79 years old and has been receiving
SLP(Crl.)  No.6410/2026                                           2

--- Page 119 ---
treatment from Chirayu Cancer Hospital. He submits that
his condition is an advanced and life-threatening stage
of  cancer,  which  requires  continuous  and  specialized
medical  treatment.  Further,  he  submits  that  he  has
already served 16 years 5 months and 11 days of his
sentence, with remission.
5. We are of the opinion that the age and physical condition
of the Petitioner makes this a fit case for grant of
continuance on parole to the Petitioner. The Petitioner
be released from custody forthwith and shall continue to
remain on parole.
6. The State shall consider his case for remission and in
the meanwhile, the learned counsel for the State shall
produce a report of the conduct of the Petitioner during
his incarceration, before this Court. The Registry shall
forward  the  above  order  to  the  appropriate  State
authorities for consideration of remission.
7. The matter shall be posted on 26.10.2026.
  (CHANDRESH)                              (POOJA SHARMA)
  ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                  COURT MASTER (NSH)
SLP(Crl.)  No.6410/2026                                           3

--- Page 120 ---
ITEM NO.2               COURT NO.6               SECTION II-E
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No.6410/2026
[Arising  out  of  impugned  final  judgment  and  order  dated
20-01-2026 in WA No. 155/2026 passed by the High Court of
Madhya Pradesh at Indore]
PREM SINGH                                     Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF M.P & ANR.                           Respondent(s)
IA No. 109400/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Vaibhav Srivastava, Adv.
                   Ms. Sugandha Anand, AOR
                   Mr. Chetan Anand Mohapatra, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Ms. Manisha T. Karia, Sr. A.A.G.
                   Mr. Abhinav Srivastav, Adv.
                   Mr. Pashupathi Nath Razdan, AOR
                   
                   
       UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
SLP(Crl.)  No.6410/2026                                           4

--- Page 121 ---
                             O R D E R
1. The Petitioner sought extension of parole on medical
grounds, specifically for his treatment for Carcinoma
Prostate with Bone Metastasis. The Petitioner is now
suffering also from complications arising after surgery.
2. The  Petitioner  was  convicted  by  Sessions  Court
Byabra,  District  Rajgarh,  MP  for  offences  punishable
under Sections 147, 148, 342, 302 read with 149, and 323
read with 140 of Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short “the
IPC”) vide judgment dated 19.12.2001 in Sessions Case No.
12/2000 and has been inter alia imposed with a life
sentence.
3. The petitioner approached the High Court through a
Writ Petition and was allowed special parole between the
period from 08.08.2025 to 10.12.2025 which was extended
from time to time on medical grounds. Madhya Pradesh High
Court in order dated 10.10.2025 in WP No.40084/2025 had
extended the parole for a period of 2 months with the
stipulation that no further extension or parole under any
circumstance shall be granted to the Petitioner.
4. The Petitioner is 79 years old and has been receiving
treatment from Chirayu Cancer Hospital. He submits that
SLP(Crl.)  No.6410/2026                                           5

--- Page 122 ---
his condition is an advanced and life-threatening stage
of  cancer,  which  requires  continuous  and  specialized
medical  treatment.  Further,  he  submits  that  he  has
already served 16 years 5 months and 11 days of his
sentence, with remission.
5. We are of the opinion that the age and physical
condition of the Petitioner makes this a fit case for
grant of continuance on parole to the Petitioner. The
Petitioner shall not hence be directed to surrender and
shall continue on parole.
6. The State shall consider his case for remission and
in the meanwhile, the learned counsel for the State shall
produce a report of the conduct of the Petitioner during
his incarceration, before this Court. The Registry shall
forward  the  above  order  to  the  appropriate  State
authorities for consideration of remission.
7. The matter shall be posted on 26.10.2026.
(CHANDRESH)                              (POOJA SHARMA)

in IA 220152/2025  Mr. Senthil Jagadeesan,Sr.Adv.

                   Ms. Niharika Shukla,Adv.                   
                   Mr. Sajal Jain, AOR
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr.  Senthil  Jagadeesan,  learned  senior
counsel appearing for the intervenor (IA No.220152/2025)
and Mr. Gaurav Sharrma, learned senior counsel appearing
for the respondent-National Medical Commission (NMC).
2. Learned  senior  counsel  for  the  respondent-NMC
submits that the Guidelines on Assessment of Persons with
Benchmark  Disabilities  (PvBD)  For  Admission  to  MBBS
Course, 2026 (for short, "the Guidelines") which had gone
for vetting,  have been duly vetted and now it is in the
process of being sent to the Director General, Health
Services (DGHS). Learned senior counsel assures the Court
that the Guidelines will be included in the prospectus of
NEET  Under  Graduate  Brochure  for  2026-2027  which  is
likely to be issued very shortly.  Learned senior counsel
also submits that the Guidelines for Under Graduate will
also be uploaded on the website.
2

--- Page 131 ---
3. Mr.  Senthil  Jagadeesan,  learned  senior  counsel
drawing  attention  to  page  4  paragraph  10  of  the
additional  affidavit  dated  14 th July,  2027  of  the
respondent No.1-Union of India submits that admittedly as
of now only 33 notified Disability Assessment Boards are
available.  The affidavit states that is is proposed to
be increased to 43.  Learned senior counsel points out
that as per the judgment of this Court, there should be
minimum of one Disability Assessment Board in each State
and each Union Territory.  The affidavit of the Union of
India is not clear on this aspect of the matter. 
4. Let a better affidavit be filed by the respondent
No.1-UOI  setting  out  the  correct  position  and  also
explaining whether the direction of this Court has been
complied with.
5. Learned senior counsel for the intervenor further
points out that under the directions of this Court each
Disability Assessment Board must include a doctor or
health professional with disability.  Drawing attention
to page 5 para 13 of the affidavit of the union of India
dated 14th July, 2026, learned senior counsel submits that
all that is set out is that steps are being taken. The
matter pertains to the rights of individuals under the
Right of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 and also
about enforcement of the orders of this Court. By the
next date of hearing, the Union of India will set out the
3

--- Page 132 ---
composition  of  each  Disability  Assessment  Board  and
ensure that there is in each of the Board a doctor or
health professional with disability.
6. On the aspect of training and sensitization of
members of the Disability Assessment Boards, as directed
by this Court in its judgment in Om Rathore v. Director
General of Health Services and Others 1, the affidavit is
completely silent.  The Union of India by the next date
of hearing will explain and set out their stand on this
aspect of the matter.
7. Learned senior counsel submits that with regard to
the constitution of Appellate Body, the direction in
Onkar Ramchandra Gond  v.  Union of India & Ors. 2 is to
constitute Appellate Body at the level of the Director
General of Health Services to hear challenges.  What is
suggested is that conclusion of one Disability Assessment
Board  be  reviewed  laterally  by  another  Disability
Assessment  Board.  This is  not the  purport of  the
judgment.  The idea was to provide a higher hierarchy.
The respondent No.1-Union of India to explain in its
affidavit as to why no steps have been taken in this
regard.
8. Learned senior counsel further points out that the
judgments of this Court have directed constitution of
enabling units at Medical Colleges to act as points of
1. 2024 SCC OnLine SC 3130
2. 2024 SCC OnLine SC 2860
4

--- Page 133 ---
contact for persons with disabilities seeking clinical
accommodations and to inform students of the same.  The
affidavit is completely silent on this aspect.
9. The matter pertains to the Rights of Persons under
the and it is a very serious matter.  The respondent
No.1-Union of India is directed to bestow its highest
attention and file a detailed affidavit so that the
directions  of this  Court are  complied with  and are
operational for the Academic Year 2026-2027 itself.
10. List the matter on 28th July, 2026 at 2.00 p.m.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.669 OF 2025
  YOGA PRIYA. S                                    PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
 MANJUNATH I                                      RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. The present Transfer Petition has been filed under
Section 25 of the Code of Civil Procedure seeking transfer
of M.C. No.95 of 2024, titled “ Manjunath I. Vs. Smt. Yoga
Priya S.”, pending before the Court of the learned Senior
Civil Judge and JMFC at Shikaripura, Karnataka, to the
learned Sub Court at Thiruchendur, Thoothukudi, Tamil Nadu.
2. As per the Mediation Report dated 15.05.2026, the
Mediation inter se the parties is successful.
3. Learned counsel for the parties submitted that all the
disputes and differences between the parties have been
amicably resolved, and the parties have decided to live
together. Accordingly, appropriate and necessary orders may
be passed in accordance therewith.
4. We appreciate the efforts put in by Mr. V. K. Biju,
learned Mediator, Supreme Court Mediation Centre, and also
learned counsel for the parties in helping the parties
1

--- Page 135 ---
arrive at an amicable settlement. We also deeply appreciate
the parties for taking the decision to live together.
5. The terms of the Settlement Agreement dated 02.05.2026
reads as under:
“1.  The  Parties  agree  to  resume  cohabitation  as  
husband and wife and to live harmoniously in the best  
interests of their minor child, M.Tanush Mugilan.
2.  That the parties here by mutually agreed that the 
Respondent husband shall meet the Petitioner wife and 
the child at Tirunelveli Junction Railway Station within
one week from the date of disposal of the aforesaid 
Transfer Petition by this Hon'ble Court, in view of the 
present settlement agreement. That it is further agreed 
that the parties shall accordingly proceed together to 
the matrimonial home.
3.  It is agreed that both parties shall reside together
at the matrimonial home in Shikaripura Taluk, Shivamogga
District, Karnataka.
4.  It  is  agreed  between  the  parties  that  the  
petitioner wife shall with draw forthwith following  
litigations/complaints,  and  all  proceedings  arising  
against the respondent husband and his all relatives. 
The Petitioner Wife shall cooperate in proceedings for 
quashing FIR No.24/2025 dated 2 August 2025, registered 
under Section 85 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, 
and Section 4 of the Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961, at the
All Women Police Station, Srivaikuntam, Thoothukudi,  
before the Madurai Bench of the Hon'ble Madras High  
Court.
5.  It is agreed between the parties that the Respondent
Husband shall withdraw M.C. No. 95 of 2024 pending  
before  the  Hon'ble  Senior  Civil  Judge  and  JMFC,  
Shikaripura.
6. Both Parties shall withdraw all other criminal  
Complaints filed against each other within 3 months of 
this Agreement.
7.  Both Parties undertake not to harass, intimidate, or
hold past conduct against each other, and shall treat 
each other with respect and dignity.
8.  The  Respondent  Husband  shall  provide  suitable  
accommodation with essential amenities, ensuring the  
2

--- Page 136 ---
well being of the child.
9.  The  Respondent  Husband  shall  also  provide  a  
supportive environment for the Petitioner Wife to  
education and employment, extending pursue financial
and moral support as needed.
10. Both Parties shall jointly ensure the child's  
physical, emotional, and educational welfare.
11. The Respondent Husband shall ensure that the  
Petitioner Wife has personal space within the joint 
family setup and arrange proper accommodation ensuring 
privacy of Petitioner Wife.
12.  The Petitioner Wife under takes to reside with the
Respondent Husband in the matrimonial home and make 
reasonable adjustments to the living arrangements  
provided. Neither Party shall compel the other to meet 
or speak with relatives, including parents or family 
members, against their will.”
6. In view of the above, the following directions are
issued:
i)  It goes without saying that both the parties will
continue to abide by the terms of the settlement
without any exception;
ii) The terms of the Settlement Agreement are binding
upon the parties. The parties undertake to abide by
the same; and
(iii)  The  parties,  through  their  learned  counsel
present  in  Court,  have  been  made  aware  of  the
consequences of breach of such terms, including the
initiation of contempt proceedings.
7. Accordingly, the Transfer Petition is disposed of.
3

--- Page 137 ---
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

(SANJAY KAROL)

petitions).
3. The present petitions calls in question the correctness
of the common order dated 28.11.2025 in Criminal Miscellaneous
No.51534/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Patna.
By the said order, the High Court enlarged the aforesaid
private respondent, on bail pending trial.
4. Ms.Katju, learned Senior Counsel and Mr.Khan, learned
counsel, contend that the High Court has not adverted to any
of the parameters required for consideration for enlargement
of the bail in a serious and heinous offences. According to
the prosecution, the private respondent -Mukesh Paswan was the
rider of the motor bike in which the assailant  Prince was
seated as  pillion. Learned Senior Counsel/counsel for the
petitioner(s) contends that recoveries have been effected at
the behest of the said private respondent and further that the
investigation reveals that call details records (CDR) which
indicate that the private respondent - Mukesh Paswan was seen
in the crime scene at the time of the occurrence. They also
contend that there are criminal antecedents.
5. They further contends that none of these facts have been

--- Page 142 ---
4
adverted to by the High Court and the High Court has enlarged
the private respondent -Mukesh Paswan on bail.
6. Learned counsel for the private respondent - Mukesh Paswan
vehemently defence the impugned order and contends that the
bail order is made on 28.11.2025 and that the trial is in
progress and six witnesses have been examined.
7. Having  carefully  considered  the  contentions  of  the
parties and perused the records, we are of the opinion that
the High Court has not applied the parameters laid down by
this Court for enlargement of parties on bail in heinous
offences i.e. the private respondent - Mukesh Paswan being the
alleged rider of the bike from which the co-accused Prince
riding as pillion shot the deceased; discovery at the behest
of private respondent - Mukesh Paswan and the CDR details as
revealed in investigation.
8. Learned counsel for private respondent - Mukesh Paswan
has  asserted  that  these  are  the  allegations  of  the
prosecution, which have to be established in trial. Learned
counsel  for  private  respondent  - Mukesh  Paswan  denies
involvement of Mukesh Paswan in the commission of offence.
9. Having considered the relevant factors, we are of the
opinion that the impugned order(s) cannot be sustained.
10. Accordingly, the prayer of petitioner(s) is allowed. W e
set  aside  the  impugned  order  and  direct  that  private
respondent -Mukesh Paswan shall surrender to custody within a
period of two weeks from today.
11. In case there is a default on the part of  private

--- Page 143 ---
5
respondent -Mukesh Paswan to surrender, we direct the State to
take him into custody. 
12. With these observations, the Special Leave Petitions are
disposed of.
13. All pending applications including the application(s) for
permission to appear & argue shall stand disposed of. 
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (MANOJ KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

--- Page 154 ---
Non-Reportable
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal No..............of 2026
[ @ Special Leave Petition (Crl.) No.9340 of 2025 ]
Purushothaman Nair ....Appellant
Versus
The District Collector and Ors. ....Respondents
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant is  aggrieved  by  the criminal  prosecution
initiated for offences under Sections 420, 421, 427, 465, 468,
471 read with Section 34 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (IPC)
by  FIR  No.1181  of  2016  dated  04.11.2016  of  Kuthiyathode
Police Station against eight persons which has now culminated
in a chargesheet produced as Annexure P4.
3. The appellant prays that the above chargesheet may be
quashed since the allegation is of a civil wrong and even if the
allegations are accepted in its entirety, it will not attract any of
the alleged offences. A learned Single Judge of the High Court
of Kerala, rejected the claim, finding that even if the facts give
rise  to  a  civil  claim,  if  it  also  amounts  to  an  offence  then
necessarily a criminal prosecution can be initiated. It was found
Crl.A @ SLP (Crl) No.9340 of 2025 Page 1 of 5

--- Page 155 ---
that in the present case, a settlement was registered by the
appellant  herein,  the  accused,  in  favour  of  his  son  without
disclosing  the  fact  that  there  are  other  claimants  to  the
property, specifically the 3rd respondent who claims through his
father.  There  is,  hence,  a  dishonest  concealment  of  facts
leading to deception which constitutes cheating under Section
415 of the IPC, was the finding.
4. On facts, it is to be noticed that the appellant and the
complainant, the 3 rd respondent, are the son and grandson of
two sisters respectively. The sisters, belonging to one branch of
a  Tharavadu,  obtained  the  ancestral  house,  standing  in  15
cents of land as per a partition in the year 1115 ME, in common
with the other members of the branch. The appellant’s mother,
one of the sisters, settled the property in the name of the
appellant, allegedly suppressing the fact that there were other
claimants to the property. The appellant followed it up with a
settlement deed in the year 2014, again on the same premise
that his aunt, the sister of his mother, died issue less.
5. In fact, the appellant’s aunt had a son; the father of the 3rd
respondent. The contention was that the deceit was employed
to frustrate the claim of the complainant. It is also alleged that
the settlement deed would not have been  registered  if the
Crl.A @ SLP (Crl) No.9340 of 2025 Page 2 of 5

--- Page 156 ---
claim of the other family members were noticed; we cannot but
say a very slippery premise.
6. If the property devolved in common to the members of a
branch, the execution of a settlement deed in exclusion of the
co-owners would not defeat the claim of the co-owners. Such a
contention can only be raised by a third party who purchased
the property, if at all. The 3 rd respondent’s remedy is to file a
civil suit against the settlement deed, if he so desires.
7. We also notice that the learned Single Judge has recorded
that one other person has filed a suit to restrain the appellant
and  his  son  from  demolishing  the  residential  building  to
construct a new one.
8. We are of the opinion that there is absolutely no reason to
permit  the  criminal  prosecution  to  be  continued.  The  Final
Report at Annexure P4 has only laid a charge as against the 1 st
accused. It is trite that the Magistrate is competent to even
raise  a  charge against  the  other  accused,  if  on  a  judicious
consideration, it is found possible from the materials available.
We notice this since only the appellant who is the 1 st accused
has filed the quash petition. But, we find no reason to proceed
against the other accused also since the allegation essentially
is of deceit employed by the 1 st accused, which we find to be
Crl.A @ SLP (Crl) No.9340 of 2025 Page 3 of 5

--- Page 157 ---
misplaced.  The  other  accused  are  also  not  culpable.   We,
hence, quash the captioned FIR and Annexure P4 Final Report
filed.  The Judicial Magistrate of First Class-II, Cherthala shall
desist  from  proceeding  with  the  prosecution  and  close  the
matter.
9. The appeal stands allowed.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
   ……….……………………. J.
                                                        (J.B. PARDIWALA)         
 
                                         ………….…………………. J.
                                                             (K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 21, 2026.
Crl.A @ SLP (Crl) No.9340 of 2025 Page 4 of 5

--- Page 158 ---

Leave granted.
The  appellant  seeks  anticipatory  bail  in
connection  with  FIR  No.332  of  2024,  dated
09.09.2024,  registered  at  Police  Station  –
Shhivaji  Nagar,  District  –  Gurugram  for  the
offences punishable under Sections 420, 467, 468,
471 and 120-B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860.
We have heard the learned Senior counsel and
the learned counsel appearing for the parties.

--- Page 160 ---
2
Learned  Senior  counsel  appearing  for  the
appellants submitted that it is a civil dispute
inter se the parties and the civil suit filed at
the instance of the complainant is pending.  The
appellants have joined the investigation, pursuant
to the order dated 23.03.2026.  
Learned counsel appearing for the respondent-
State submitted that though the appellants have
joined  the  investigations,  they  are  not
cooperating with the same.
Having heard the parties, we are inclined to
take into consideration the fact that it is a
civil dispute inter se the complainant and the
appellants and the appellants have joined the
investigation.
Suffice it is to state that on the facts of
the instant case, the custodial interrogation of
the appellants is not required.
In such view of the matter, we are inclined
to grant anticipatory bail to the appellants.

--- Page 161 ---
3
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellants are granted anticipatory
bail on terms and conditions to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court.
We make it clear that the appellants shall
continue  to  join  and  cooperate  with  the
investigation till its conclusion.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO(S). 8646/2011
NAYEEM SAB @ NAYEEM BASHA & ANR.                   APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
S. J. GNANACHANDRA                                 RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. We have heard Mr. Amit Pai, learned counsel for the
appellant(s).
2. No one appears for the respondent, despite service
of notice to make alternative arrangements.
3. In a suit for recovery of a sum of Rs.2,10,446/-
with interest @ 24 per cent per annum, preferred by the
respondent-plaintiff, the Trial Court dismissed the suit,
however, the first Appellate Court under the impugned
judgment and decree has allowed the first appeal and
decreed the suit to the extent of Rs.61,000/- with interest
@ 15 per cent per annum.
4. Plaintiff preferred the suit on the plea that he has
supplied  paddy  to  the  defendants  on  16.01.2000  worth
Rs.1,22,000/-, for repayment of which the defendants issued
1

--- Page 175 ---
two cheques of Rs.61,000/- each, dated 05.02.2000 and
02.02.2000 respectively, which was presented for encashment
but was dishonored due to “funds found insufficient” in the
defendants’ bank account. Thereafter, the plaintiff served
legal notice and preferred the present suit.
5. According to the appellants-defendants, they have
paid a sum of Rs.50,000/- in cash to the plaintiff’s
brother and deposited another sum of Rs.61,000/- in the
account of S.J. Girish at Vijaya Bank, at the time when the
plaintiff’s father was admitted to hospital for treatment.
6. Thus, the defendants admit to a sum of Rs.11,000/-
remaining to be paid to the plaintiff. The Trial Court
framed issue nos.1 to 3 with burden of proof on the
plaintiff and issue no.4 with burden of proof on the
defendants.  This  particular  issue  no.4  was  regarding
whether the defendant No.2/appellant has proved that he is
liable to pay only Rs.11,000/- towards supply of paddy
transaction.
7. The Trial Court found that neither the plaintiff nor
the defendants have proved their case and thus, dismissed
the  suit.  The  High  Court,  although  by  a  very  small
judgment, but with a sound reasoning, held that once the
defendants have admitted the plaintiff’s case of supply of
paddy to them with a specific plea that defendants have
repaid Rs.50,000/- in cash and Rs.61,000/- by depositing
the amount in the account at the time of plaintiff’s
father’s illness, the defendants admit the transaction.
2

--- Page 176 ---
8. We are also of the considered view that once the
defendants admit the transaction by raising a specific plea
that though defendants have received the paddy but have
repaid the amount of Rs.50,000 + Rs.61,000/- and owes only
Rs.11,000/- to the plaintiff, and if the same has been
found unproved by the defendants, as a necessary corollary,
once the supply is proved, the plaintiff’s claim that no
amount has been paid to him after supply of paddy is to be
accepted, to the extent High Court has found under the
impugned judgment. Learned counsel for the appellant has
relied on “Santosh Hazari vs. Purushottam Tiwari1”. There is
no quarrel with legal proposition that the first Appellate
Court, while exercising power under Section 96 of Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908 should deal with all the issues on
which the parties went on trial, however, since the present
lis pertains to the principal sum of Rs.1,22,000/- only and
the High Court has decreed the suit only to the extent of
Rs.61,000/- and we have independently examined the material
on  record,  we  are  not  inclined  to  remit  the  matter.
Accordingly, the appeal fails and is hereby dismissed.
9. Accordingly, we do not find any substance in this
appeal except to the extent of grant of interest to the
plaintiff @ 15 per cent per annum. In the present Price
Index Scenario, ordinarily Courts are allowing interest @
6-9 per cent per annum only. Therefore, we modify the
judgment and decree passed by the High Court, by directing
1 . (2001) 3 SCC 179
3

--- Page 177 ---
that the plaintiff-respondent shall be entitled to interest
@ 8 per cent per annum on the decreetal sum of Rs.61,000/-.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of. 
  
 ………………………………………………………J.
             [PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA]
   
    ………………………………………………………J.
           [SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR]
NEW DELHI;
July 15, 2026
4

--- Page 178 ---

ITEM NO.4               COURT NO.13               SECTION II-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  7766/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  04-10-2025 in BA
No. 1173/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay at
Aurangabad]
SHRIKANT DNYANOBA GITTE                            Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA & ANR.                    Respondent(s)
IA No. 107144/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 22-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Amol Nirmalkumar Suryawanshi, AOR
                   Ms. Damini Vishwakarma, Adv.
                   Mr. B. Dhananjay, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Varad Kilor, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddharth Dharmadhikari, Adv.
                   Mr. Aaditya Aniruddha Pande, AOR
                   Mr. Shrirang B. Varma, Adv.
Mr Anurag Gharote, Adv.(through VC)*
                   
                   Mr. Shreyas Gacche, Adv.
                   Mr. T. R. B. Sivakumar, AOR
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Amol Nirmalkumar Suryawanshi , learned counsel
for the petitioner and Mr. Shreyas Gacche, learned counsel for
defacto complainant and Mr. Anurag Gharote, learned counsel for
the State.
2. The present petition calls in question the correctness of
the order dated   04-10-2025 in BA No. 1173/2025 passed by the
High Court of Judicature at Bombay at Aurangabad. By the said

--- Page 180 ---
order, the prayer for bail of the petitioner was rejected.
3. When the matter came up for hearing on 29.05.2026, this
Court enlarging the petitioner on interim bail, made the
following order:-
“1.  Heard  Mr.  Amol  Nirmalkumar  Suryawanshi,  learned
counsel for the petitioner, Mr. Anurag Gharote, learned
counsel for the State and Mr. Shreyas Gacche, learned
counsel for the complainant. 
2. When this matter came up for hearing on 15.04.2026,
the following order was passed by this Court:
“1. Heard Mr. Amol Nirmalkumar Suryawanshi, learned
counsel appearing for the petitioner. 
2. Delay condoned.
3. At the very outset, Mr. Amol Suryawanshi, learned
counsel, after obtaining instructions and on the own
volition of the petitioner, submits that according to
the petitioner, the balance amount payable to the
complainant  is  Rs.21,00,000/-  (Rupees  twenty  one
lakhs). Learned counsel submits that already a sum of
Rs.29,00,000/- (Rupees twenty nine lakhs) has been
deposited in the Court and for an amount equivalent
to Rs.10,00,000/- (Rupees ten lakhs), a land parcel
has been transferred in the name of the complainant.
Apart from this, another amount of Rs.10,00,000/-
(Rupees  ten  lakhs)  has  been  transferred  in  the
account of the complainant. That leaves the balance
of Rs.21,00,000/- (Rupees twenty one lakhs) which the
petitioner on his own volition submits that he will
deposit the balance amount within a month in the
Trial Court.
4. Issue notice subject to the condition that the
petitioner  deposits,  as  undertaken,  a  sum  of
Rs.21,00,000/- (Rupees twenty one lakhs) with the
Trial Court on or before 14th May, 2026.

--- Page 181 ---
5.  Mr.  Varad  Kilor,  learned  counsel  appears  and
accepts notice on behalf of the respondent-State.
6. List the matter on 18th May, 2026.”
3. In compliance thereof, the amount of Rs.21,00,000/-
has  been  deposited  in  the  concerned  Trial  Court.
According to learned counsel for the petitioner, the
complainant in this matter is secured for the amount of
Rs.70,00,000/- with the deposit of Rs.21,00,000/-.
4. Last time, when the matter came up for hearing on
18.05.2026, the State had taken time to file a status
report/counter affidavit. Till today, nothing is filed.
5. The petitioner is in custody for about 02 years.
6. Under these circumstances, we are inclined to enlarge
the petitioner on interim bail.
7. Accordingly, the petitioner is enlarged on interim
bail subject to the satisfaction of the District &
Sessions Judge-2, Nanded, District Nanded, Maharashtra
(for short, ‘Sessions Court at Nanded’), in connection
with  Crime  No.03/2024  (Special  Case  No.26/2024)
registered with Shivaji Nagar Police Station, Nanded,
District Nanded, Maharashtra for the offences punishable
under Sections 406, 409, 420, 467, 468, 471 & 506 of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 and under Sections 3 & 4 of the
Maharashtra Protection of Interest of Depositors (in
Financial Establishments)Act, 1999.
8. The Sessions Court at Nanded inter alia impose a
condition to the effect that the petitioner will report
at the local Police Station once in a week.
9. The amount lying in deposit in the concerned Trial
Court  shall  be  kept  in  a  short  term  FDR  in  a
Nationalized Bank on auto renewal basis.
10. List the matter on 22nd July, 2026.
11. In the meantime, a last opportunity is given to the
State to file a status report/counter affidavit.”
4. We have also seen the status report/counter affidavit

--- Page 182 ---
filed by the State pursuant to the said order.
5. Considering the fact that the petitioner has undergone
about two years before he was released on interim bail and that
post the release of interim bail, there have been no complaints
against the petitioner, we are inclined to confirm the order
dated 29.05.2026 granting interim bail to the petitioner.
6. The petitioner shall continue to be on bail on the same
terms during the pendency of the trial.
7. Learned counsel the defacto- complainant submits that the
amount so deposited before the concerned Trial Court, should be
released to the defacto-complainant.
8. Learned counsel for the State submits that there are
other aggrieved parties.
9. We  say  nothing  on  this  aspect  here.  The  defacto-
complainant is at liberty to file an application before the
concerned Trial Court and the said Court may keeping in mind
all  the  factors  involved,  make  an  appropriate  order  in
accordance with law.
10. With the above observations, the special leave petition
is disposed of.
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.

Non-Reportable 
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal No.           of 2026
(@  Special Leave Petition (Crl.) No.2388 of 2026  )  
Mithu Ghosh and Anr.  ....Appellants
Versus
The State of West Bengal & Anr.   ....Respondents
O R D E R 
Leave granted.
2. We have a strange situation here, where the case of
two accused who had not petitioned the High Court, was
considered by the High Court in the petition filed under
Section 482 for quashing the FIR by the other accused.
3. The  High  Court  having  dealt  with  two
petitioners/appellants herein found that the charge against
the  appellants  under  Section  3(1)(s)  of  the  Schedule
Castes and Schedule Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act,
19891 would  stand  quashed  but  the  proceedings  under
Section 3(1)(r) of the said Act would be continued.
1  For brevity, “the SC&ST Act”
Page 1 of 4
SLP (Crl.) No.2388 of 2026 

--- Page 188 ---
4. The crime case was registered based on FIR No.1024 of
2023  dated  15.12.2023  registered  at  Matigara  Police
Station under Sections 447/323/354B/506 and 34 of the
Indian Penal Code, 18602 r/w Sections 3(1)(r) and 3(1)(s) of
the SC&ST Act.  However, the findings of the High Court
leading to continuance of the prosecution under the other
provisions cannot be sustained.  To that extent, the order
is  set  aside.   The  appellants  are  left with  remedy  to
approach the High Court under Section 482 for quashing
the entire proceedings, if they so desire, which shall be
considered  untrammeled  by  the  observations  in  the
impugned judgment.
5. We  make  it  clear  that  we  have  not  made  any
comments on the merits of the matter. 
6. The appeal stands disposed of with the above liberty.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
EXTRAORDINARY APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Special Leave Petition (C) No  s  .17945-17948 OF 2026  
Bharathidasan University,
Rep. by its Registrar  …Petitioner
Versus
The Joint Commissioner
of GST (ST-Intelligence)
Trichy Division & Anr.                           …Respondents
O R D E R
The petitioner is a University concerned with a show cause
notice issued under Section 74(5) of the Tamil Nadu Goods and
Services  Tax  Act,  2017  raising  a  demand  of  GST  along  with
applicable interest and penalties on the affiliation fees collected by
the University from its affiliated institutions, for the financial years
2019-2020  to  2022-2023.  The  petitioner  relied  on  Notification
No.12/2017 - Central Tax (Rate), which provides that educational
and regulatory levies do not constitute a commercial ‘supply’ or
‘consideration’. On conflicting views being expressed by two Single
Benches  of  the  High  Court  in  similar  challenge,  the  matter was
referred to the Division Bench.  
2. The Division Bench concurred with one of the decisions holding
that the service of granting affiliation is an independent statutory
Page 1 of 4
SLP(C) No.17945-17948 of 2026

--- Page 192 ---
pre-requisite falling outside the restricted scope of ‘services relating
to  admissions  to,  or  conduct  of  examination  by  an  educational
institution’.  Having answered the reference, the operative portion of
the impugned judgment indicates that the matter was remanded to
the learned Single Judge to decide the residual issues.
3. Sri S. Nagamuthu, learned Senior Counsel appearing for the
University submits that there are conflicting decisions from various
High Courts and this Court had dismissed an appeal filed by the
department against a decision of the High Court of Karnataka, which
held an exemption to be applicable even with respect to affiliation
fees. It is submitted that for an expeditious consideration of the
question  which  is  recurring  and  mulcting  huge  liability  on  the
Universities in this Country, it is only appropriate that this Court
considers the matter of exemption.
4. We notice that the order dated 24.01.2025 passed in SLP (C)
D. No.59470/2024 (annexure P9 of the present SLP), in which this
Court dismissed the SLP does not result in a merger of the decision
with this Court as has been held in  Kunhayammed and Ors. v.
State of Kerala and Anr.1 Since the matter has been remanded to
the learned Single Judge to consider the residual grounds, we are of
the opinion that let the matter be considered by the learned Single
Judge and then it would be open for the petitioner to challenge any
1  (2000) 6 SCC 359
Page 2 of 4
SLP(C) No.17945-17948 of 2026

--- Page 193 ---
order prejudicial to the petitioner along with the decision of the
Division Bench in the reference, before this Court.
5. We also make it clear that in the meanwhile if this Court holds
in  favour  of  the  assessee,  then  necessarily  petitioner  would  be
entitled to produce such decision before the learned Single Judge,
which would on terms overrule the finding rendered on reference by
a Division Bench of the High Court.
6. With the above reservation and observation, the Special Leave
Petitions are closed  with  liberty  reserved  to  the  petitioner  to
challenge the reference order after disposal of the matter before the
learned Single Judge.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
.……….…….……….……. J.
                   (J.B. PARDIWALA)
.……….…….……….……. J.
     (K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI
JULY 16, 2026.

SLP(C) No.17945-17948 of 2026

--- Page 195 ---
Non-Reportable
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Civil Appeal No..............of 2026
(@Special Leave Petition (C) No.21232 of 2022)
Gurmail Kaur and Ors. ....Appellants 
Versus
National Insurance Company 
Limited & Ors. ....Respondents
O R D E R
Leave granted.
2. The claimants are in appeal before this Court from an order
passed by the High Court of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh in
appeal  reversing  the  judgment  of  the  Motor  Accidents  Claims
Tribunal,  Sangrur and finding 100% negligence  on the  injured-
deceased in a motor vehicle accident.
3. The Tribunal had granted a total amount of compensation of
Rs.82,65,850/-  with  7.5%  interest  per  annum.  The  amount  of
compensation  was  apportioned  between  the  four  claimants  as
seen from the award. The High Court in an appeal filed by the
insurer of the offending vehicle, a truck, found that the deceased
was  crossing  the  road  and  was  hence  totally  negligent,  thus
interdicting a claim by the dependents.
1

--- Page 196 ---
4. We heard Mr. Divyansh Mishra, learned counsel appearing
for the  appellants and Ms.  Meenakshi Midha, learned Standing
Counsel for the respondent-National Insurance Company Limited.
5. The controversy is narrow since the High Court found, on the
basis of deposition of the eyewitness, that the deceased jumped
over a diversion wall on the National Highway which was found to
be total negligence on the part of the deceased. The eyewitness
was  the  person  who  was  riding  the  motorcycle  in  which  the
deceased was riding pillion.  The pillion rider was dropped off on
the side of the National Highway and while he was crossing the
road, the accident occurred. In fact, the specific statement of the
eyewitness was that the deceased tried to cross the divider on the
National Highway when a truck driven rashly and negligently hit
him, injuring him fatally. There is a world of difference between a
divider and a diversion wall.  The divider on the National Highways
are put up for keeping the traffic in check, while crossing a divider
when walking across the National Highway cannot be said to be a
negligent act. The High Court wrongly assumed that the deceased
scaled  a  wall  to  drop  down,  on  to  the  Highway  while  he  just
stepped over a divider.
6. In the above circumstances, we are of the opinion that the
High Court erred egregiously and we set aside the order of the
High Court, restoring the order of the Tribunal.
2

--- Page 197 ---
7. The  Insurer  shall  pay  the  amounts  to  the  claimants,  as
awarded  by  the  Tribunal  within  two  months  from  today  with
interest as directed by the Tribunal.
8. The appeal stands allowed.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
.……….……………………. J.
                                                         [J.B. PARDIWALA]
 
                                            .……….……………………. J. 
                                                              [K. VINOD CHANDRAN]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 16, 2026.
3
