Non-Reportable
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
EXTRAORDINARY APPELLATE JURISDICTION
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.11962 OF 2026
RAGHURAJ PRATAP SINGH    PETITIONER
VERSUS
BHANVI KUMARI SINGH   RESPONDENT
O R D E R 
The  petitioner  and  the  respondent  are  locked  in  an
acrimonious matrimony and resulting litigation, one of which
is under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act,
2005 (PWDV Act) is the subject matter of the above SLP.
2. The  controversy  is  narrow  insofar  as  whether  the
proceedings taken before the special designated MP/MLA Court
at Rouse Avenue, would be maintainable for lack of jurisdiction
especially considering the statutory mandate under territorial
jurisdiction under Section 27 of the PWDV Act.
3. Originally the matter was filed before the Metropolitan
Magistrate-02 (Mahila Court), South District, Saket Courts, New
Delhi,  which  was  later  withdrawn  and  the  Special  Court
approached, allegedly for expeditious disposal.  The High Cout
of Delhi  has affirmed  the detailed  order passed  on various
1
SLP(Crl.) No.11962 of 2026

--- Page 204 ---
decisions  of  this  Court  in  Ashwini  Kumar  Upadhyay  v.
Union of India and Another 1.  Having gone through the one
of the reports in  (2021) 20 SCC 613 , we are of the opinion
that  the  Special  Courts  for  MP/MLA  were  sought  to  be
designated to try criminal cases against MPs/MLAs in offences
under the Indian Penal Code (IPC) and the special legislations.
Large pendency and delay occasioned, prompted this Court to
pass orders directing designations of special Courts to ensure
public  accountability,  promote  vigilance  and  prevention  of
corruption.
4. We are not convinced that a matrimonial dispute can be
agitated before such a special Cour merely for reason of one of
the parties being a legislator.
5. Be that as it may, we are not called upon to decide the
question  of  law  since  the  respondent  who  was  present-in-
person was more concerned about the expeditious disposal of
the case.  The learned counsel for the parties gave consent to
transfer  the  case  to  the  court  where  it  was  first  filed  i.e.
Metropolitan  Magistrate-02  (Mahila  Court),  South  District,
Saket Courts, New Delhi.
1  W.P.(C) No.699/2016
2
SLP(Crl.) No.11962 of 2026

--- Page 205 ---
6. We make it clear that the observations made by us are
merely prima facie and the question of law is kept open.  On
consent of the parties, it is directed that the case ( CT Case
No.12/2024  titled  ‘Ms.  Bhanvi  Kumari  Singh  v.  Sh.
Raghuraj  Pratap  Singh’)  now  pending  before  the  Chief
Metropolitan  Magistrate;  Rouse  Avenue  Courts,  shall  stand
transferred  to  the  Metropolitan  Magistrate-02  (Mahila  Court),
South District, Saket Courts, New Delhi.  We request the MM-02
Court to explore the possibility of disposal of the case within at
least an year and the parties are directed to co-operate.
7. The Special Leave Petition stands disposed of.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
.................................J.
   [J.B.PARDIWALA]
.................................J.
   [K. VINOD CHANDRAN]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 15, 2026
3
SLP(Crl.) No.11962 of 2026

--- Page 206 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        /2026
      [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.4295/2026]
ROHIT KUMAR @ ROHIT             Appellant(s)
                              VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA                Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The  appellant  seeks  anticipatory  bail  in
connection with FIR No.18/2026, dated 22.01.2026,
registered  at  Police  Station  –  Parao,  Ambala
Cantt.,  District  –  Ambala  for  the  offences
punishable  under  Section  115(2),  126(2),  190,
191(3), 351(3) and 110 of the Bharatiya Nyaya
Sanhita, 2023.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
We have been informed that the appellant has
joined the investigation pursuant to our order
dated 12.03.2026.  He is not involved in any other
criminal case as stated in the counter affidavit
1

--- Page 208 ---
filed by the State.
Considering the above, we are of the view
that custodial investigation of the appellant is
not required.
In such view of the matter, we are inclined
to grant anticipatory bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellant is granted anticipatory
bail on terms and conditions to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
...............….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
 CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION   
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 643 OF 2022
LEMON FUTERMAL JAIN                                Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
NEOGROWTH CREDIT PVT. LTD. & ORS.                  Respondent(s)
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 658 OF 2022
O R D E R 
1. The petitioner has filed two petitions under Section 406 of
the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 before this Court seeking
transfer of cases detail of which are as under: 
a)  Complaint Case No. CS/31818/21 titled as “NeoGrowth Credit Pvt.
Ltd.  vs.  M/s  Tarun  Polymers”  pending  before  the  Court  of
Metropolitan Magistrate, 7th Court, Calcutta to District Court
Complex, District Thane, Maharashtra.
b) Complaint  Case  No.  CS/62987/19  titled  as  “Magma  Fincorp
Limited vs. M/s Tarun Polymers & Ors.” pending before the Court of
Metropolitan Magistrate, 8th Court, Calcutta to District Court
Complex, District Thane, Maharashtra.
2. Despite due service of notice, no one has entered appearance
on behalf of the respondent(s).
1

--- Page 216 ---
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and taking
into consideration the grounds taken in the transfer petitions and
more particularly the principles laid down in the case of Golla
Naraesh Kumar Yadav etc. vs. Kotak Mahindra Bank , reported in 2025
INSC 1387, we allow the transfer petitions and direct the transfer
of cases  detail of which are as under: 
a)  Complaint Case No. CS/31818/21 titled as “Neo Growth Credit
Pvt. Ltd. vs. M/s Tarun Polymers” pending before the Court of
Metropolitan Magistrate, 7th Court, Calcutta to District Court
Complex, District Thane, Maharashtra.
b) Complaint  Case  No.  CS/62987/19  titled  as  “Magma  Fincorp
Limited vs. M/s Tarun Polymers & Ors.” pending before the Court of
Metropolitan Magistrate, 8th Court, Calcutta to District Court
Complex, District Thane, Maharashtra.
4. Let the record of the case be transferred without delay.
5. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned court for
compliance. 
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

   [PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA]

                   Mr. Rohit Khurana, Adv.
                   Mr. Alsamad Qureishi, Adv.
                   Ms. Akshara Santosh, Adv.
                   Mr. Suvansh Pathak, Adv.
                   Mr. Kumar Harsh, Adv.
                   Mr. Divakar Kumar, AoR
  
For Respondent(s) :M/S. Equity Lex Associates, AoR
                   Mr. Salman Khurshid, Sr. Adv.
                   Mrs. Naghma Imtiaz, Adv.
                   Mr. Zargham Ahmed, Adv.
                   Mr. Saif Naseem, Adv.
                   Ms. Mariya Mnasuri, Adv.
                   Ms. Sidra Khan, Adv.
  
  
                             O R D E R
Heard Ms. Kamini Jaiswal, learned counsel for the petitioner  
and Mr. Salman Khurshid, learned Senior Counsel appearing for the  

--- Page 220 ---
respondent.
2. This Court had directed the parties to remain physically  
present. Pursuant thereto, the petitioner, her parents, respondent  
No.2, the petitioner’s husband and their son are present before us.  
We have interacted with the petitioner, her husband and their son  
separately, thereafter, jointly, and finally with all the parties  
present along with their learned counsel. 
3. At the outset, we place on record not only our appreciation  
but also our gratitude to the learned Senior counsel/counsel  
appearing for the parties, who have brought them to a stage where  
it appears that an amicable settlement may be possible, which  
would, at the very least, bring an end to the animosity prevailing  
between the parties. 
4. The  core  issue  is  that  the  petitioner  has  been  living  
separately from her husband and son for more than twelve years and  
now wishes to return to the matrimonial home and restore a normal  
mother-son relationship with her son, who is presently about  
seventeen years of age. The principal impediment, however, is the  
multiplicity of proceedings pending inter se between the parties,  
including  a  divorce  petition  instituted  by  the  petitioner’s  
husband, a petition for restitution of conjugal rights filed by the  
petitioner, and certain criminal cases. 
5. Having interacted with the parties, we record that it has been  
mutually agreed that as an initial step, a psychologist shall  
counsel and facilitate interactions amongst them with a view to  
restoring normalcy in their relationship. We accordingly appoint  
Dr.  Preeti  Sethi,  Clinical  Psychologist,  Trauma  Healer  and  
Relationship Counsellor, whose name has been jointly suggested by  

--- Page 221 ---
the learned counsel for the parties, to act as the counsellor.
6. Accordingly, let the first counselling session be held at the  
clinic of Dr. Preeti Sethi at 1:30 p.m. on 21.07.2026. Thereafter,  
since the respondents do not ordinarily reside in Delhi and the son  
is also residing outside Delhi, the subsequent counselling sessions  
may be conducted through the virtual mode. At this stage, we  
consider it appropriate that the counselling process continue for a  
period of at least three months, whereafter the counsellor shall  
submit her assessment/report to this Court so that appropriate  
further directions, as required, may be considered. We also record  
that the parties have been sufficiently sensitized during the  
course of interaction and have agreed, in principle, that all  
disputes pending  inter se  deserve to be amicably resolved and  
finally concluded.
7. We also note that during our interaction, the petitioner  
consistently maintained that her primary desire is to restore a  
normal and meaningful mother-son relationship. She expressed her  
wish that her son should respond to her telephone calls, remain in  
regular communication with her, keep her informed about significant  
developments in his life and, subject to mutual convenience, meet  
her from time to time. We record that in principle, not only the  
son and the petitioner’s husband but also the other family members  
have expressed that this would not pose any difficulty for them. 
8. Before parting with this matter, we earnestly request all the  
parties to make sincere endeavours to evolve a mutually acceptable  
arrangement so that cordiality and mutual trust may be restored  
within  the  family.  We  have  also  indicated  that  if  such  an  
understanding is ultimately arrived at, all proceedings pending  

--- Page 222 ---
inter se should be brought to a quietus. During our interaction,  
both sides, particularly at the intervention and persuasion of  
their son, agreed in principle that they would take appropriate  
steps for the closure of all proceedings instituted by either side  
against the other.
9. List the matter on 06.11.2026 at 2.00 p.m. Before the next  
date of hearing, Dr. Preeti Sethi is requested to submit a report  
indicating the progress and the current status of the counselling  
process. As regards her professional fee, the learned counsel for  
the parties shall, in consultation with Dr. Preeti Sethi, determine  
an appropriate honorarium, which shall be shared equally by the  
parties. Initially, the counselling shall be confined to the  
petitioner, her husband and their son. However, it shall be open to  
Dr. Preeti Sethi to decide whether the participation of any other  
family member is necessary for an effective counselling process.  
The parties have also indicated that they shall remain personally  
present before this Court on the next date of hearing.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
   COURT MASTER (SH)                          ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
ARBITRATION PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 41 OF 2025
VEENA GARG PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
ADITYA KUMAR HALWASIYA RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This petition under Section 11(6) of the Arbitration and
Conciliation Act, 1996 (“Act”), seeks the appointment of a sole
arbitrator to adjudicate disputes arising from the Share Purchase
Agreement dated 08.09.2023, executed between the Petitioner and the
Respondent.
2. Briefly observed, the Petitioner is part of the promoter group
of M/s. Cupid Limited, a public limited company incorporated in
Nashik, India involved in the manufacture of contraceptives and
other auxiliary products. The Respondent is the Managing Director
of Cupid Limited.  As part of the business transaction, through a
Share Purchase Agreement dated 08.09.2023, the Petitioner (and
other promoters) agreed to sell some shareholding in Cupid Limited
to the Respondent and other buyers, for total sale consideration of
Rs. 109.6 crores.
3. It  appears  that  a  dispute  arose  between  the  parties,
especially on the claim of the Petitioner that the Respondent
failed to adhere to his regulatory obligations under the Share
1

--- Page 232 ---
Purchase Agreement. Furthermore, it is alleged that the Respondent
did not intend to actually conclude the transaction or comply with
the regulatory necessities. In such circumstances, the Petitioner
sought to initiate arbitral proceedings in accordance with Clause
8.3 of the Share Purchase Agreement. We may note that Clause 8
provides for the resolution of disputes between the parties by a
Sole Arbitrator at Mumbai, India under the Act. 
4. The Petitioner, thus, issued notice invoking arbitration dated
10.03.2025 to the Respondent, suggesting the name of Justice A.A.
Sayed, former Chief Justice of the Himachal Pradesh High Court, for
appointment as the Sole Arbitrator. However, the Respondent has
objected to the arbitration as well as the suggested arbitrator.
Hence, the Petitioner has filed the instant Petition under Section
11 of the Act.
5. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and perused the
material placed on record.
6. Keeping in view the facts and circumstances recorded above and
the relevant provisions under the agreement dated 08.09.2023, we
deem it just and proper to allow the instant Arbitration Petition
and appoint Justice Shiavax Jal Vazifdar, former Chief Justice of
the Punjab & Haryana High Court, as the Sole Arbitrator to resolve
the  disputes  between  the  parties.  The  seat  and  venue  of
arbitration, in accordance with the arbitration agreement, shall be
Mumbai, India.
2

--- Page 233 ---
7. The Respondent shall be at liberty to raise the question of
arbitrability and maintainability as well as the jurisdiction issue
before the learned Arbitrator. It is clarified that we have not
expressed any opinion with respect to the same.
8. The  Arbitrator  shall  have  the  liberty  to  fix  his
remuneration/fees. Ordered accordingly.
9. Pending  interlocutory  applications,  if  any,  also  stand
disposed of.
..........................CJI.
       (SURYA KANT)
..........................J.
       (JOYMALYA BAGCHI) 
..........................J.
       (V. MOHANA)                     
 
NEW DELHI;
JULY 17, 2026.
3

--- Page 234 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL  NO.         OF 2026
(@ SLP(C) No.33141/2025)
COLLECTOR LAND ACQUISITION HPPWD & ORS.            APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
CHHAVINDER SINGH & ORS.                            RESPONDENT(S)
WITH
CIVIL APPEAL  NO.         OF 2026
(@ SLP(C) NO. 3085/2026)
CIVIL APPEAL  NO.         OF 2026
(@ SLP(C) NO. 810/2026)
CIVIL APPEAL  NO.         OF 2026
(@ SLP(C) NO. 5189/2026)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard the learned counsel appearing for the
appellant(s) and the learned counsel appearing for the
respondents.
3. Learned counsel appearing for the respondents submitted
that there is a huge delay in filing the appeal before
the High Court.
4. The issue raised in the instant appeals is covered by the
judgment of this Court in C.A. Nos.909-910/2026 and
connected matters, titled as ‘The Deputy Commissioner and

--- Page 240 ---
2
Special Land Acquisition Officer Vs. M/S S.V. Global Mill
Limited’ decided on 09.02.2026.
5. Even in cases of a higher delay in filing the appeal
before the High Court, we have allowed such appeals by
condoning the same. 
6. In such view of the matter, we are inclined to set aside
the impugned order in terms of the aforesaid judgment.
7. Accordingly, the impugned order(s) stand set aside and
the appeals are allowed.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
  
  ……………………………………………………………………J.
  [M.M. SUNDRESH]
       ………………………………………………………………………J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;

1. Heard Mr. Navin Pahwa and Ms. Kaadambari Singh,
learned senior counsel appearing for the petitioners and
Mr. Maninder Singh, learned senior counsel appearing for
respondent No.1. The impugned order is a consent order.
2. Delay condoned in Diary No.14180 of 2026.
3. On 30th January, 2026, when we issued notice in SLP
(Civil)  No.3574 of  2026, we  wanted to  be satisfied
whether  there  was  justification  for  non-payment  of
interest for the period 2010-2021, as claimed by the
petitioners herein.  
4. Today, we have heard the learned senior counsel for
respondent No.1.  Respondent No.1 has explained to us the
details  of  the  original  agreement,  the  events  that
2

--- Page 257 ---
happened in the interregnum and ultimately, the events
that led to the consent order in Court. We have seen the
consent order duly signed in accordance with law and in
the presence of the learned Judge.
5. We  see  no  good  ground  to  interfere  with  the
impugned  order.   The  Special  Leave  Petitions  are
accordingly dismissed.
6. Pending applications shall also stand disposed of.

ITEM NO.22               COURT NO.13               SECTION II
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  12342/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated 13-04-2026 in
A528BNSS No. 2815/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
SURAJ KUMAR DAS                                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P. & ANR.                               Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 194369/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Satyam Pandey, Adv.
                   Mr. Raghvendra Upadhyay, AOR               
                   Ms. Purnima Jain, Adv.
                   Ms. Shivpa Taneja, Adv.
                   Mr. Madhur, Adv.
                   Mr. Swaraj Maurya, Adv.
                   Mr. Avdhesh Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Chandra Kishore Yadav, Adv.
                   Mr. Awadhesh Kumar, Adv.
                   Ms. Kajal Verma, Adv.
                   Mr. Siya Ram Sharma, Adv.
                   Mrs. Gayatri Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Saurabh Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Dilip Kumar, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Satyam Pandey, learned counsel appearing
for the petitioner.
1

--- Page 259 ---
2. Learned counsel submits that the respondent No.2
was  originally  convicted  under  Section  138  of  the
Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (for short, “the Act”)
and sentenced to two years simple imprisonment and a fine
of Rs.13,00,000/-.  It was further directed that out of
the fine amount, a sum of Rs.12,50,000/- was to be paid
to the complainant and the remaining sum of Rs.50,000/-
was to be deposited as fine in favour of the State
Government.  Default sentence of four months of simple
imprisonment  was  also  imposed.   The  respondent  No.2
carried the matter to the court of Sessions in appeal by
filing Criminal Appeal No.168 of 2025.  
3. The Sessions Judge, by order dated 12 th November,
2025, passed an order in accordance with Section 148 of
the  Act  directing  that  on  deposit  of  20%  of  the
compensation amount of Rs.12,50,000/- within a period of
60 days, the sentence of the respondent No.2 will stand
suspended.   
4. This  order  was  carried  to  the  High  Court  by
respondent  No.2  under  Section  528  of  the  Bharatiya
Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023. T he High Court, while
modifying the direction to make 20% of the fine amount,
to that of 10% of the fine amount as deposit, has
directed that on deposit of 10% of the fine amount, the
entire proceedings arising out of Complaint Case No.503
of 2024 shall be kept in abeyance.  
2

--- Page 260 ---
5. Learned counsel has a two-fold submission, (i) in
no circumstance could the entire proceedings be kept in
abeyance, and; (ii) that the direction to reduce the
deposit from 20% to 10% is contrary to the judgment of
this Court in the case of  Surinder Singh Deswal  v.
Virender Gandhi1
6. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner, we
are of the opinion that till the next date of hearing, in
the event of respondent No.2 complying with the order of
the High Court, his sentence shall remain suspended.
7. However, we direct that the Sessions Judge shall
proceed with the hearing of Criminal Appeal No.168 of
2025.
8. List the matter on 16th September, 2026.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)

8322/2026

STATE OF ODISHA                             Respondent(s)

Date : 22-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing 
today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Shyam Manohar (arguing Counsel), Adv.
                   Mr. Ambrish Kumar, Adv.

      UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr. Shyam Manohar,  learned counsel for the
petitioner and  Mr. Srisatya Mohanty,  learned counsel
for the State.
2. The  present  petition  calls  in  question  the

--- Page 262 ---
2
correctness of the order  dated  17-02-2026 in BLAPL
No.1101/2026 passed by the High Court of Orissa at
Cuttack. By the said order, the prayer for bail of the
petitioner was rejected in connection FIR No.192/2023
registered  with  Padampur  Police  Station,  District
Baragarh/Bargarh, Odisha, corresponding to Special GR
Case No.57-B/2023 for the offences punishable under
Sections 20(b)(ii)(C)/29 of the Narcotic Drugs and
Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 (for short, ‘NDPS’)
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that
according to the prosecution, the recovery was from the
building belonging to  accused - Chhuta @ Indrajeet/
Indrajit  Mehar.  Learned  counsel  submits  that
prosecution has set up a case that the land belongs to
the  petitioner.  Learned  submits  that  the  land  is
already  stood  transferred,  however,  taking  the
prosecution’s  case  at  its  highest  no  conscious
possession is attributed to the petitioner. Learned
counsel also submits that two other accused A4- Surat
Mehar @ Subrat Mehar and A6- Sesha @ Seshadev Mehar
have been enlarged on bail. Learned counsel further
submits that the petitioner has undergone nearly 14
months in custody.
4. Learned counsel for the State submits that aforesaid
accused ( A4 & A6) are not on par with the petitioner
because there is no allegation against them that the
land belonged to them.

--- Page 263 ---
3
5. Keeping the overall facts and circumstances, the
period  undergoes;  the  nature  of  allegation;  other
accused A4- Surat Mehar @ Subrat Mehar and A6- Sesha @
Seshadev Mehar being on bail and also bearing in mind
the twin condition of Section 37 of NDPS, we are of the
opinion that the petitioner has made out a case for
bail.
6. Accordingly, we direct that pending trial, the
petitioner shall be enlarged on bail subject to the
satisfaction of the Trial Court concerned in connection
with  the  aforesaid  Case  No.192/2023  and  for  the
offences mentioned hereinabove.
7. As learned counsel for the State submits that the
petitioner was at large for more than a year and hence,
stringent condition need to be imposed.
8. The concerned Trial Court may keep this fact in mind
and impose stringent condition.
9. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                             (MANOJ KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                  COURT MASTER (NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
 
 
CIVIL APPEAL NO. 7118 OF 2015 
 
 
 
U.P. RAJKIYA NIRMAN NIGAM LIMITED                APPELLANT(S) 
 
 
                                VERSUS 
 
 
YOGENDRA SINGH & ORS.                           RESPONDENT(S) 
 
 
 
O R D E R 
 
 
 
1. The correctness of the judgment rendered by the High Court 
of Judicature at Bombay dated 10.06.2015, affirming the award 
passed by the Industrial Tribunal, Pune in Reference (IT) No. 
06 of 2003, is the subject matter of the present appeal. The 
Industrial Tribunal had directed absorption of the respondents 
into the permanent service of the appellant, with consequential 
benefits. 
2. It is not in dispute that the appellant  i.e. U.P. Rajkiya 
Nirman Nigam Limited, is a State Government undertaking engaged 
in civil construction and design of buildings across India. For 
execution and maintenance of projects at Pune, Chinchwad, and 
Kolhapur, the appellant engaged local contractors. The 
respondents, alleging unfair labour practice and continuous 
engagement for several years without permanency benefits, 

--- Page 274 ---
2 
pleaded direct employment under the appellant. 
3. The defence of the appellant before the Tribunal and the 
High Court was that the respondents were employees of 
contractors, and that the appellant could not be treated as an 
“establishment” under the Model Standing Orders. Be that as it 
may, the High Court, while affirming the Tribunal’s award, 
noted the continuous service rendered by the respondents under 
the direct supervision of the appellant’s officials and held 
that their rights deserved protection. 
4. Having considered the contentions advanced and the factual 
matrix borne out of the record , it is evident that a 
significant number of respondents have since superannuated 
during the pendency of this litigation. Records indicate that 
Respondent No. 1 (Yogendra Singh), Respondent No. 2 (Aman 
Kumar), and Respondent No. 10 (Motilal Trimbke), among others, 
have already attained the age of superannuation. 
5. In these circumstances, while we are not inclined to 
interfere with the impugned judgment , it is clarified that the 
benefit of permanency shall be confined to disbursement of 50% 
of the wages of permanency, payable to the respondents until 
they reach the age of superannuation (60 years). Those 
respondents who have already superannuated shall be entitled to 
such benefits only up to the date of their retirement. 
6. With the above modification, Civil Appeal No. 7118 of 2015 
stands disposed of. 
 
 

--- Page 275 ---
3 
 
 
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed 
of. 
 
 
        ........................J. 
                   (SANJAY KAROL)     
 
 
 
 
         ...........................J.  
                                    (NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH)     
 
NEW DELHI; 
JULY 16, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
CIVIL APPEAL No. 9994 OF 2011
U.P.PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION THROUGH 
ITS CHAIRMAN APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
SHAILENDRA SINGH & ANR.                            RESPONDENTS
WITH
CIVIL APPEAL No. 9995 OF 2011
   
O R D E R
1. An adjournment is prayed on behalf of the learned counsel appearing for
the appellant – Uttar Pradesh Public Service Commission 1 on the ground that he
is in personal difficulty today.
2. Two issues arise for decision in these appeals, which are directed against
the common judgment and order dated 13 th August, 2008 of the High Court of
Judicature at Allahabad. They are: (i) whether the principles of reservation as
per roster were correctly applied by the UPPSC and (ii) whether the High Court
could have set aside the selection of Dr. Permanand T. Dudhey without he being
a respondent in the writ petition filed by the first respondent.
3.  We find that the order allowing the writ petition of the first respondent
was stayed by this Court on 27th March, 2009; hence, the directions given by the
High Court could not be implemented.
4. The stay has been continuing for the last 17 (seventeen) years and, in the
1  UPPSC

--- Page 283 ---
2
meanwhile, all the private parties must have retired from service. 
5. We also find from the office report that despite service of notice, the first
respondent has not entered an appearance. It is, thus, evident that he too is
disinterested in contesting the appeals.
6. Much water having flown under the bridge since the order was stayed, the
issues  raised  in  these  appeals  noticed  above  have  now  become  academic.
Deciding the issues, one way or the other, will make no practical difference to
the parties anymore.
7. In such view of the matter, without expressing any opinion on the merits
of the claim raised in the appeals by the appellants but keeping the question of
law open, the appeals stand disposed of.
8. Pending interlocutory application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
……….............................J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]
………..............................J.
[SHEEL NAGU]
New Delhi;
July 21, 2026.

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No(s).7073/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 04-12-2023
in CRLA No. 19/2011 passed by the High Court of Orissa at Cuttack]
SURENDRA SA                                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF ODISHA                                Respondent(s)
IA No. 103832/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 103833/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Ms. Kumud Lata Das, AOR
                   Mr. Harsh Ajay Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Bibhuti Krishna, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashutosh Saini, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Akshay Amritanshu, AOR
                   Mr. Sarthak Srivastava, Adv.
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner was convicted for the offence under
Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (IPC) and was
sentenced to imprisonment for life.
2. Having  gone  through  the  appellate  judgment;  the
judgment of the trial court having not been produced, we
1

--- Page 289 ---
SLP (Crl.) No.7073/2026
are of the opinion that there is no reason to interfere
with the conviction awarded based on the eyewitness’s
testimony,  the  dying  declaration;  spoken  of  by  two
witnesses and the postmortem report to indicate the death
by homicide and injuries in consonance with the ocular
testimony.
3. The petitioner had committed the murder of a person,
an eyewitness in an earlier trial for the same offence
tried as SC No.130/15 of 2006. We inquired with the learned
counsel appearing for the petitioner as to how the earlier
trial culminated; which was responded as having ended in a
conviction.
4. The overall circumstances restrain us from granting
any indulgence to the petitioner/accused. However, the
petitioner would be entitled to seek for remission and if
such an application is made, the same shall be considered
under the remission policy of the State Government. We
direct  the  Superintendent  of  the  jail  in  which  the
petitioner is imprisoned to extend legal assistance by
availing the services of the State/District/Taluk Legal
Services Committee.
5. We make it clear that we have not made any observation
on the remission which have to be considered as per the
policy of the State Government as also reckoning the
conduct of the petitioner in having murdered a person who
had been an eyewitness in an identical crime committed by
2

--- Page 290 ---
SLP (Crl.) No.7073/2026
the petitioner on an earlier occasion.
6. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of with the
above directions.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
(HARPREET KAUR)                                 (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO(s).      OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO(s).8757 OF 2026)
CHANDAN KUMAR @ CHANDAN RAJ                        APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR                                 RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
25.02.2026 in Criminal Miscellaneous No.14996/2026 passed
by the High Court of Judicature at Patna.
3 We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
perused the material available on record.
4. We have noticed the nature of the crime and the
manner in which it was allegedly committed.
5. It is not in dispute that the pistol and empty
cartridges  were  not  recovered  from  the  conscious
possession  of  the  appellant  but  from  the  rented
accommodation of the co-accused.
6. In view of the same, we are inclined to interfere
with  the  impugned  judgment  and  order  and  grant
anticipatory bail to the appellant. The impugned judgment
and  order  dated  25.02.2026  is  set  aside.  We  grant
anticipatory bail to the appellant.
7. In  the  event  of arrest  in  connection  with F.I.R.
No.370 of 2025,  registered  at  Parsa  Bazar  Thana
1

--- Page 292 ---
Police Station, Patna District, Bihar, the appellant
shall be released on bail by the Investigating/Arresting
Officer on such terms and conditions as may be imposed
and found to be just, fair and reasonable.
8. However, in the attending facts, one of the conditions
shall necessarily be that if the appellant possesses a
passport,  the  same  shall  be  deposited  before  the
Competent Authority/Court, until the conclusion of the
trial or unless directed otherwise.
9. The appellant shall make himself available before the
Investigating Officer on 22.07.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and on
all such dates as he may be required.
10. Needless to add, until the investigation is complete
in all respects, the appellant shall fully cooperate, and
if the challan/charge sheet is filed, he shall maintain
good conduct and not attempt to influence any of the
witnesses in any manner till the completion of the trial.
11. Accordingly, the present appeal is allowed.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

infructuous unless the exemption from surrendering is granted.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Civil Appeal No.1555/2016
ARCHANA BANSAL & ORS.              APPELLANTS
VERSUS
DIRECTORATE OF EDUCATION & ANR.            RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the appellants and learned
counsel appearing for the respondent-Institution.
2. We  have  also  perused  the  impugned  judgment  and  order  dated  17th
August, 2015 passed by the Division Bench of the High Court of Delhi at New
Delhi1, refusing to interfere with the decision of the respondent-Institution not to
consider  the  claim  of  the  appellant  (widow)  of  K.K.  Bansal  (the  deceased
Principal) favourably for release of balance 50% of the wages due to him for the
period from 01st April, 2002 to 22nd July, 2003. 
3. It is  found on perusal of the  material on record  that the  respondent-
Institution had given an opportunity to K.K. Bansal to produce documents to
prove  that  he  was  not  in  gainful  employment  during  the  period  of  his
unemployment (from 01st April, 2002 to 22nd July, 2003) but he refused to do so.
Even then, the respondent-Institution was generous enough to grant K.K. Bansal
50% of the back wages.
4. Although,  it  is  true  that  in  course  of  subsequent  proceedings  the
appellants sought to produce the income tax returns of K. K. Bansal for the
1 High Court
1

--- Page 298 ---
relevant assessment years, the High Court took into consideration the fact that
he  was  imparting  tuition  classes  and,  therefore,  earning  through  self-
employment. The rule of no work-no pay was, accordingly, applied by the High
Court in refusing relief. 
5. We do not see reason to take a view different from the one taken by the
High Court. The impugned judgment and order is upheld.
6. The civil appeal is, accordingly, dismissed.
7. We record that the respondent-Institution has very fairly accepted the
request of the Court and agreed to pay Rs.1,00,000/- (One lakh rupee) only to
the  first appellant  (widow)  of  K.  K.  Bansal  as  soon  as  she  approaches  the
Principal of the respondent-Institution with a copy of this order.
8. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.
..........................................J.
(DIPANKAR DATTA)
...........................................J.
(SHEEL NAGU)
New Delhi;
July 21, 2026.
2

--- Page 299 ---

ITEM NO.32               COURT NO.4               SECTION II
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  21259/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  08-09-2025
in CRLR No. 918/2024 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
JUVENILE X (MINOR)                                 Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P & ORS.                                Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE M.M. SUNDRESH
         HON'BLE  MR. JUSTICE PRASANNA B. VARALE
For Petitioner(s) Mr. Lokesh Kumar Choudhary, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) Ms. Ruchira Goel, AOR
                   Ms. Ritika Rao, Adv.
                   Mr. Sharanya, Adv.
                   Ms. Rishika Rishabh, Adv.
                   Ms. Krithi Ballal, Adv.
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the parties.
The issue raised in the present petition is
covered by the decision of this Court in  Barun
Chandra Thakur v. Master Bholu And Another  – (2023)
12 SCC 401 wherein it has been held as under:
“85. We are conscious of the fact that the
power to make the preliminary assessment is
vested in the Board and also the Children’s

--- Page 301 ---
2
Court  under  Sections  15  and  19
respectively.  The Children’s Court, on its
own, upon a matter being referred to under
Section 18(3), would still examine whether
the child is to be tried as an adult or
not, and if it would come to the conclusion
that the child was not to be tried as an
adult  then  it  would  itself  conduct  an
inquiry as a Board and pass appropriate
orders under Section 18.  Thus, the power
to carry out the preliminary assessment
rests with the Board and the Children’s
Court.  This Court cannot delve upon the
exercise of preliminary assessment.  This
Court will only examine as to whether the
preliminary assessment has been carried out
as required under law or not.  Even the
High Court, exercising revisionary power
under Section 102, would test the decision
of the Board or the Children’s Court with
respect to its legality or propriety only.
In the present case, the High Court has,
after remanded the matter to the Board with
further  directions  to  take  additional
evidence  and  also  to  afford  adequate
opportunity to the child before taking a
fresh decision.”
In view of the law laid down as aforestated, the
said exercise undertaken by the Juvenile Justice
Board is only a preliminary one and, therefore,
there is no bar for the Children’s Court to go into
the said issue once again, extensively.
In such view of the matter, the Special Leave

--- Page 302 ---
3
Petition stands disposed of by giving liberty to the
petitioner to have recourse before the Children’s
Court,  in  which  case  the  order  passed  by  the
Juvenile Justice Board will not stand in the way.
Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of. 
(ASHA SUNDRIYAL)                                (POONAM VAID)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                             ASSISTANT REGISTRAR 

--- Page 303 ---
ITEM NO.8               COURT NO.9               SECTION II-E
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  855/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  15-09-2023  
in CRA No. 1239/2016 passed by the High Court of Gujarat at  
Ahmedabad]
MAHESHKUMAR DHISALAL JANGID                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT                                   Respondent(s)
IA No. 308948/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) :Ms. Mahalakshmi Pavani, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Sumit Chander, Adv.
                   Mr. Gurdeep Chauhan, Adv.
                   Mr. Ajeet Yadav, AoR
                   Mr. Sonakshi Chaturvedi, Adv.
                   Mr. Prabhash Kumar Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Neeleshwar Pavani, Adv.
                   Ms. Ragini Juneja, Adv.
                   Mr. Abhishek Upadhayay, Adv.
                   Ms. Prabisha Pradeep, Adv.
                   Ms. Neha Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Davinder Kumar Rawat, Adv.
  
  
For Respondent(s) :Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, AoR
                   Ms. Aditi Agarwal, Adv.
  
  
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner and learned counsel  
for the State of Gujarat. 
2. The challenge in the present Special Leave Petition is to the  
order of the Gujarat High Court by which the conviction and  

--- Page 304 ---
sentence of the petitioner has been upheld.
3. As the petitioner had undergone more than 14 years of actual  
incarceration, we had called upon the State to consider his case  
for premature release under the relevant policy of the State  
Government and take a final decision.
4. The affidavit filed on behalf of the State dated 02.04.2026  
has been placed on record by which the prayer of the petitioner for  
premature release has been rejected vide Government of Gujarat’s,  
Home Department order dated 01.04.2026 No. JLK/832026/V.J.75/J.
5. Learned Senior Counsel for the petitioner submitted that such  
rejection is totally against the settled principles of law and that  
various relevant factors in the present case have totally been  
ignored by the Authorities concerned. It was further submitted that  
the petitioner needs to be given the benefit of the 1992 policy,  
whereas his case has been considered under the 2014 policy. 
6. Per contra, learned counsel for the State submits that the  
1992 policy has ceased to be in force. According to her, the policy  
of 2014, which came into force prior to the conviction of the  
petitioner by the Trial Court, which was on 27.01.2015, is the only  
policy which is existing and under which the case had to be  
considered, which has been done. It was submitted that in terms of  
this policy, the petitioner's case could not have been favourably  
considered and the aforesaid detailed order dated 01.04.2026 has  
been passed.
7. Be that as it may, we find that even if there are issues which  
may be arguable, both on facts and law with regard to the order  
dated 01.04.2026, in the present proceeding, the same are not  
required to be gone into, as the prayer for premature release of  

--- Page 305 ---
the petitioner has been rejected by a substantive order in which  
reasons have been assigned. Thus, it has to be assailed in a duly  
constituted proceeding before the appropriate Court/Forum.
8. For the reasons aforesaid, we are not persuaded to interfere  
with the impugned order passed by the High Court.
9. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition is disposed of, with 
liberty to the petitioner to assail the order dated 01.04.2026 of  
Government of Gujarat relating to the rejection of his prayer for  
premature release. If the same is done, the Court/Forum concerned  
shall consider the same on its own merits, without being prejudiced  
by the present order.
10. Pending application(s), if any, also stand disposed of.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
   COURT MASTER (SH)                          ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

--- Page 306 ---
1

The  appellants  stand  convicted  for  the
offences punishable under Sections 148, 294(b),
307, 506, 324 and 302 read with Section 34 of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 by the High Court. They
have been sentenced to undergo imprisonment for
life. It is a case of reversal and the appellants
have been under incarceration for a period of five
years and eleven months.

--- Page 308 ---
3
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
Considering the above, we are inclined to
grant bail, during the pendency of the appeals, to
the appellants on terms and conditions to the
satisfaction of the Trial Court.
The applications stand allowed, accordingly.

Mr.  Kapil  Chandel  (Email  ID:  kapilchandel014@gmail.com  and

--- Page 310 ---

Enrolment No.D/18089/2025) a young counsel present in the Court,

appeared for the petitioner, the learned amicus was permitted to
assist the Court.

4. Issue notice, returnable on 07.09.2026.
5. Dasti service, in addition, is permitted. Let steps for

6. In  the  notice  itself,  let  it  be  mentioned  that  the
respondent(s) is required to file the counter affidavit and reply
to the interlocutory application(s), if any, before the next date
of listing.
7. In the meanwhile, all further proceedings  inter se  the
parties, arising out of matrimonial discord, shall remain stayed.
An endeavor shall be made to have all disputes arising out of
matrimonial discord, whether pending before different forums or
under contemplation, settled amicably.
8. However, if there is any order of maintenance passed by any
appropriate authority, the same shall continue to be complied
with by the husband.
9. It  is  clarified  that  the  learned  amicus  curiae shall
continue to assist the Court as such.
10. The Registry shall print the name/names of the learned
amicus curiae in the cause list.
11. The Registry is directed to supply the complete paperbook,

Ms. Priyanka Sethia, Adv.
Mr. Shravanth Shanker, Adv.
Ms. Poonam Shekhawat, Adv.

Mr. Mukul Rohatgi, Sr. Adv.
Mr. Haris Beeran, Adv.
Mr. Akshal Malpani, Adv.
Mr. Usman Ghani Khan, Adv.                      
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard learned senior counsel/counsel for the parties.
2. In our considered opinion, no interference in the impugned
judgment of the High Court is called for. The Special Leave
Petitions are, accordingly, dismissed.  
3. However, in order to ensure that no impediment in the day-to-
day functioning is caused till the directions issued by the High
Court are complied with, paragraph No.101 of the impugned judgment
is modified to the limited extent that the Union of India (Ministry
of External Affairs) shall be at liberty to
(i) either allow the existing incumbents to provide the
subject services across all four missions till fresh RFP is
2

--- Page 356 ---
issued and work is allotted to the successful bidder(s), at
their own risk and without claiming any equity; or
(ii) temporarily allocate the work in all or any one of the
four missions to the successful bidders, whose allotment has
been  set  aside  by  the  High  Court  through  the  impugned
judgement, on the basis that this would be a temporary
arrangement at its own risk and subject to the outcome of the
fresh allocation exercise; or
(iii) evolve any other mechanism for the purpose of providing
services in the four missions in the manner as it may deem
appropriate, till the fresh allocation of work in accordance
with the directions issued by the High Court is concluded.
4. We direct the Union of India (Ministry of External Affairs) to
comply with the High Court directions as early as possible, but not
later than three months.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand closed.
(NITIN TALREJA)                            (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR
3

--- Page 357 ---
ITEM NO.2               COURT NO.16               SECTION II

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s). 8480/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 28-03-2026
in CRLMBA No. 1381/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
VIKASH KUMAR                                       Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P                                       Respondent(s)
IA No. 140997/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 142433/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Anupam Mishra, AOR
                   Mr. Jenis V Francis, Adv.
                   Mr. Harikumar V, Adv.
                   Mr. Chakma Purnojyoti, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Rajat Singh, AOR
                   Ms. Aurica Bhattacharya, Adv.
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present Special Leave Petition is for grant
of regular bail as the petitioner is in jail in
1

--- Page 358 ---
connection with FIR No. 130 of 2025, registered at
Police  Station-Purkaji,  District-Muzaffar  Nagar,
Uttar  Pradesh,  for  the  offence  punishable  under
Sections 316(2), 316(5), 318(4) of the Bharatiya
Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
2. It has been stated that petitioner was acting as
a collection agent of Bharat Finance India Limited
and after collecting the amount, he has not deposited
the same with the Company.
3. It has been brought to the notice of this Court
that  the  investigation  is  complete  and  he  has
cooperated with the investigation and the amount
involved is Rs. 2,00,000/- only.
4. Learned counsel for the respondent-State has
vehemently opposed the prayer for grant of bail.
5. This Court after careful consideration of the
record and after hearing learned counsel for the
parties, is of the opinion that the application for
grant  of  bail  deserves  to  be  allowed  and  is
accordingly allowed.
6. Accordingly, we direct that the petitioner be
enlarged on bail  in connection with abovementioned
FIR, on such terms and conditions as may be imposed
by the Trial Court, subject to the condition that the
2

--- Page 359 ---
petitioner shall  continue to co-operate with the
trial/investigation.
7. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
  (RAHUL KUMAR)                                    (KOMAL)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No(s). 3200/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 24-09-2024
in CRMM No. 47786/2024 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana
at Chandigarh]
ASHIMA ARORA                                       Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
TANVEER SINGH & ORS.                               Respondent(s)
IA No. 39975/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 39973/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Punit Vinay, AOR
                   Mrs. Anjana Gupta, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Ghan Shyam Vasisht, AOR
                   Mr. R.p Kaushik, Adv.
                   Mr. Piyush Rayal, Adv.
                   
                   Mr. Abhinav Bajaj, A.A.G.
                   Mr. Samar Vijay Singh, AOR
                   Mr. Saksham Ojha, Adv.
                   Ms. Geetashi Chandna, Adv.
                   Ms. Sabarni Som, Adv.
                   Mr. Aman Dev Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Gaj Singh, Adv.
                   
1

--- Page 361 ---
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present Special Leave Petition is arising out of
order dated 24.09.2024, passed by the High Court of Punjab
and Haryana in a petition preferred under Section 528 of
the  Bharatiya  Nagarik  Suraksha  Sanhita,  2023  in  case
bearing FIR No. 0064 dated 02.05.2024, registered at Police
Station Sector 9 Ambala, for the offence punishable under
Sections 406, 498-A and 323 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860.
2. Order  passed  by  the  High  Court  reveals  that
respondents have withdrawn the petition with a liberty to
raise all the pleas before the learned Trial Court at
appropriate stage.
3. The  contention  of  the  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner is that personal appearance of the petitioner
has been exempted.
4. This Court has carefully gone through the order and
it is true that personal appearance of the petitioner has
been exempted, however, clause 5 of he conditions reveal
that they shall appear before the Trial Court as and when
required and therefore, this Court does not find any reason
to interfere with the impugned order passed by the High
Court.  Accordingly,  the  Special  Leave  Petition  stands
dismissed.
2

--- Page 362 ---
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
  (RAHUL KUMAR)                                    (KOMAL)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

--- Page 363 ---

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 06-01-2026
in CRADB No. 1600/2025 passed by the High Court of Jharkhand at
Ranchi]
RAJ KISHORE PRASAD @ RAJ KISHORE MEHTA             Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF JHARKHAND                             Respondent(s)
IA No. 170823/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 170824/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 170822/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Kumar Manish, Adv.
                   Ms. Deepika Mishra, AOR
                   Mr. Abhishek Misra, Adv.
                   Mr. Pankaj Kumar Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Diwakar Awashthi, Adv.
                   Mr. Krishna Mohan Baitha, Adv.
                   Mr. Durgesh Kumar, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Anando Mukherjee, AOR
    Mr. Shwetank Singh, Adv.
    Mr. Utkarsh Anand, Adv.
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. There is no reply filed by the respondent-State of
Jharkhand.
1

--- Page 364 ---
2. This court while issuing notice had directed the
respondent-State of Jharkhand to file a detailed affidavit
through a senior police official indicating the progress of
criminal case arising out of FIR No. 230 dated 09.12.2023.
3. In the present case, the record reveals that against
the petitioner, crime was reported in the year 2003 and his
successive anticipatory bail petitions were dismissed. FIRs
were filed against him in the years 2016, 2020, 2022 and
2023.
4. In spite of large number of FIRs registered against
the  petitioner,  he  was  not  arrested  and  under  these
circumstances,  this  Court  vide  order  dated  05.06.2026
directed  the  State  of  Jharkhand  to  file  a  detailed
affidavit through a senior police official, indicating the
progress in the criminal case arising out of FIR No. 230
dated 09.12.2023.
5. It is really unfortunate that no affidavit has still
been filed in the matter.
6. As the order passed by this Court has not been
complied with, this Court is left with no other option but
to direct personal appearance of the Superintendent of
Police, District Hazaribagh, Jharkhand. He is directed to
appear before this court on 27.07.2026.
7. In case a detailed affidavit as directed vide order
dated 05.06.2026 is filed by the Superintendent of Police,
District Hazaribagh, Jharkhand on or before next date of
2

--- Page 365 ---
hearing, his personal appearance shall be treated to have
been exempted by this Court.
8. List the matter on 27.07.2026.
  (RAHUL KUMAR)                                    (KOMAL)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
Transfer Petition(s)(Civil)  No(s).  1283/2026
  NEHA CHOURASIA                                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
ANAND KUMAR                                        Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. The present petition has been filed under Section 25 of the  
Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (for short “CPC”) by the petitioner  
seeking transfer of petition in Original Suit Case No. 898 of 2025,  
titled as “Anand Kumar Vs. Neha Chourasia” pending before the Court  
of Principal Family Court, Dhanbad to the Court of Principal Judge, 
Family Court, Gautam Buddha Nagar, U.P.
2. Though served, none appears for the respondent. 
3. Having gone through the material available on record and also  
the facts of the case, we are of the view that this Transfer  
Petition deserves to be allowed. Accordingly,  Original Suit Case  
No. 898 of 2025, titled as “Anand Kumar Vs. Neha Chourasia” pending  
before the Court of Principal Family Court, Dhanbad is transferred  
to the Court of Principal Judge, Family Court, Gautam Buddha Nagar,  
U.P.
4. Records shall be sent by the Court where proceedings are  
pending to the transferee Court promptly.
5. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned courts for  
compliance.
6. The Transfer Petition stands allowed in the aforesaid terms.
1

--- Page 403 ---
7. On an application made by the parties, the Court to which the  
case is transferred shall permit the parties to appear through  
video conference on all dates except on the dates on which their  
personal presence is absolutely mandatory.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
….…………………………………………………………………J
[VIPUL M. PANCHOLI]
New Delhi 
July 17, 2026
2

preferred an application praying for the following:-

c) Pass such other Order or Orders as this Hon’ble Court
deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of 
the case.
3. Having heard the learned counsel appearing for the parties and
having gone through the averments made in the affidavit filed on
behalf of the Respondent No.4, we are inclined to grant the reliefs
as prayed for by the Enforcement Directorate.
4. We  request  the  Registrar  General  of  the  High  Court  of
Telangana  to  provide  us  with  a  list  of  Registrars(Judicial
Officers) at the earliest.
5. Once we get the necessary information from the High Court, we 
shall proceed to pass further orders.
6. Registry is directed to inform the Registrar General of the
High Court of Telangana of this order passed today at the earliest
and obtain the necessary information.
7. Post it on Friday, 24-7-2026
8. Interlocutory  Application  Nos.181984/2026,  181986/2026  and
181987/2026  are dismissed as not pressed.
 (VISHAL ANAND)                                    (POOJA SHARMA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

O R D E R
This is a petition filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908 (for short “CPC”). The petitioner is the
wife of the respondent. The petitioner has filed this Transfer
Petition seeking the following reliefs:
“(a) Transfer the case bearing Original Suit No.79 of
2025 filed under Section 13 HMA titled “ Jai
Prateek v Jyoti Singh”  before the Court of
Principal Judge, Family Court, Ranchi filed by
Respondent  to  Family  Court,  at  Gopalganj,
Bihar; and/or
(b) To pass such and further order/orders, as this
Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the
facts  and  circumstances  of  the  case  in  the
interest of justice.”
2. On perusal of the Office Report, it is noted that the
respondent has been served with the notice issued by this
Court on 17.03.2026. However, there is no representation on
behalf of the said respondent.

--- Page 439 ---
2
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and on
perusal of the material on record, we find that the petitioner
has made out a case for transfer of the aforesaid petition.
4. In the result, the Transfer Petition is allowed and the
Original  Suit  No.79  of  2025  titled  “Jai  Prateek  v  Jyoti
Singh” pending before the Court of Principal Judge, Family
Court,  Ranchi  is  ordered  to  be  transferred  to  the  Family
Court at Gopalganj, Bihar.
5. Registry to communicate this order  to the transferor
Court as well as the transferee Court for taking steps in
accordance with this order. 
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

Pawanshree Agrawal, learned counsel as amicus curiae to
assist the Court, since the petition had been filed by
the petitioner-in-person Mr. Moharram Ali.

impugned order.

available in law, he is at liberty to pursue the same.
7. As and when those civil remedies are availed, they
will be decided in accordance with law on their own
merits, uninfluenced by any of the observations herein.

assistance rendered by Mr. Pawanshree Agrawal, learned
amicus curiae.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr.  Nithyaesh  Natraj,  learned  counsel
appearing for the appellant.
2. Learned counsel submits that under the Scheme, the
effective date for coming into force of the Scheme was
19th December, 2024.  Learned counsel submits that Section
9 application under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code,
2016 was filed before the Adjudicating Authority on 19 th
September, 2024 on which date the Scheme of Amalgamation
1

--- Page 451 ---
had not come into force.  Learned counsel relies on the
judgment of this Court in Marshall Sons & Co., (India)
Limited v. Income Tax Officer 1 and, especially paragraph
14 thereto.  
3. Learned  counsel  submits  that  in  view  of  this
position, Section 9 application was maintainable and the
fora below erred in dismissing the application on the
ground  that  on  the  date  of  filing  the  Section  9
application, the entity that filed the application did
not subsist.
4. Issue notice, returnable on 24th August, 2026.
5. Liberty is granted to serve dasti additionally.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)
     DEPUTY REGISTRAR                          COURT MASTER
1. (1997) 2 SCC 302
2

--- Page 452 ---

       UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner challenges the impugned judgment and order
dated 12.08.2025 in  S.B. Criminal Misc. Fourth Bail Application
No.10103 of 2025, titled “Prabhakar Chainsingh @ Golu Vs. State of
Rajasthan through PP”,  passed by the High Court of Judicature for
Rajasthan, Jaipur, Bench at Jaipur.
2. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and perused the
material available on record.
1

--- Page 503 ---
3. Learned counsel for the respondent/State of Rajasthan submits
that, at this point of time, twenty-eight out of thirty-six
witnesses already stand examined. The remaining witnesses shall be
produced by the prosecution on such dates, as may be fixed by the
Trial Court. The statement is accepted and taken on record.
4. In the attending facts and circumstances, considering that the
accused has been behind Bars and is suffering incarceration since
15.07.2023, we expedite the trial. W e request the Trial Court to
complete the trial, preferably within a period of six months.
5. Should the trial not progress, be it for whatever reason,
other than the reason attributed to the petitioner, l iberty is
reserved to the petitioner to renew the prayer for bail before an
appropriate forum.
6. Accordingly, the present Special Leave Petition is disposed
of.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr. V.N. Subramaniam, learned counsel
for the appellant and  Mr. B. Karunakaran, learned
counsel for the State.
2. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that
the sentence imposed is four years RI. Out of the said

--- Page 511 ---
2
period  of  sentence,  the  appellant  had  already
undergone about one year.
3. Considering  the  fact  that  it  is  a  fixed
sentence;  the  period  already  undergone  by  the
appellant as also the fact that the appeal itself is
likely to take some time for hearing, we are inclined
to suspend the sentence of the appellant and enlarge
the appellant on bail pending disposal of the appeal
by this Court.
4. Accordingly, pending disposal of the appeal, we
suspend the sentence of the appellant and enlarge the
appellant on bail subject to the satisfaction of the
concerned Trial Court in connection with Old Special
Sessions Case No.88/2016 (New Special Sessions Case
No.33/2019) for the offence punishable under Section 8
of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences
(POCSO) Act, 2012.
5. The prayer for bail is disposed of in the above
terms.

                               O R D E R
1. As  per  the  Office  Report  dated  17.07.2026,  sole
respondent  stands  served,  but  none  has  entered  an
appearance on his behalf.
2. In the interest of justice, we request and appoint Mr.
Ruchil  Raj  (E.mail  ID:  advruchilraj@gmail.com;  and
Enrollment No.: D/10990/2024), learned counsel, as amicus
curiae (pro bono), to assist the Court on behalf of the
sole respondent.

--- Page 547 ---
3. The Registry is directed to supply the complete paper
book  in  digital  form  to  the  learned  amicus  curiae
forthwith.
4. Learned amicus curiae shall endeavour to get in touch
with the respondent directly, in writing, apprising him of
the pendency of the present petition; the passing of this
order; his right of engaging a counsel of choice; and his
right of being represented through a Legal Aid Counsel,
subject to eligibility.
5. Learned amicus curiae shall also endeavour to get in
touch with the Chairman of the concerned District Legal
Services Authority, who shall also have the respondent(s)
apprised of his rights to the aforesaid effect.
6. We  clarify  that  the  learned  amicus  curiae shall
continue to assist the Court, even if the respondent is
represented by a legally engaged counsel.
7. The  Registry  shall  communicate  this  order  to  all
concerned forthwith.
8. The Registry shall print the name of the learned amicus
curiae in the cause list.
9. List on 11.09.2026.

Mr. Prem Prakash, AOR                  
                   
1

--- Page 587 ---
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. IA No.132002/2026 is allowed.
2. Having heard the learned counsels appearing for both the
parties, we are not inclined to interfere with the impugned orders.
However, we make it clear that in the event of any of the
conditions being violated by the respondent(s), the petitioner /
complainant would be at liberty to move the jurisdictional Court
for  cancellation  of  the  bail  and  in  the  event  of  such  an
application being filed, the same shall be considered on its own
merits without being influenced by its earlier order of granting
bail in favour of the respondent(s).
3. The apprehension expressed by the  petitioner, namely, the
complainant that respondents are very powerful in the local area,
would leave no stone unturned to see that the trial is delayed can
be easily allayed by requesting the trial court to expedite the
trial and in the event of either of the parties were to ask for
unwarranted adjournment, the trial court would be at liberty to
discharge such advocate and appoint an Amicus or a legal aid
counsel to proceed with the matter for expeditious disposal of the
trial.
4. With the aforesaid observation, the petitions stand disposed
of.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
  (NEHA GUPTA)                                  (NIKITA SINGH)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

Heard learned counsel for petitioner and the learned counsel  
for the State of Bihar who has appeared on advance notice. 
2. Petitioner seeks bail in connection with Lauriya P.S. Case No.

been recovered from him.

--- Page 593 ---
4. On a query of the Court at this stage, as to why the  
antecedents have not been disclosed and rather a statement has been  
made that the petitioner has no criminal antecedent, learned  
counsel submits that upon instructions, such statement has been  
made.
5. We record our strong displeasure at such statement and are of  
the opinion that the petitioner should suffer the consequences  
since the fact of criminal antecedents is mentioned in the order of  
the Trial Court itself that the petitioner had several previous  
criminal cases against him.
6. Learned counsel for the State submits that the petitioner is a  
habitual offender and should not be shown any indulgence.
7. Having considered the matter, we are not inclined to allow the  
prayer for bail to the petitioner, for the present.
8. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.
9. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
   COURT MASTER (SH)                          ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

1. The present Special Leave Petition arises out of three orders
dated 10.06.2026, 15.06.2026 and 09.07.2026 passed by the High
Court of Delhi in Cont. Cas(C) No. 1043 of 2026.
2. Mr. Mukul Rohatgi, learned senior counsel appearing for the
1 of 2

--- Page 629 ---
petitioner submits that the directions in these orders have  a
serious financial implication on the petitioner/plaintiff in the
suit.  Mr.  Rohatgi  therefore  seeks  interim  direction  pending
disposal of the Special Leave Petition.
3. We are not inclined to interfere with the orders impugned
before us. We would, however, request the High Court to advance the
listing  of  the  application  for  recalling  the  order  dated
10.06.2026, which is directed to be listed on 11.09.2026 to someday
before the end of this month.  
4. With this observation, the Special Leave Petition stands
disposed of.  
5 Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

weeks to file an additional affidavit.

status report states that 54 witnesses have already been examined,
whereas  15  witnesses  have  been  discharged  from  the  list  of
witnesses.  Therefore, 62 witnesses are left to be examined.

witnesses have already been examined, and only 6 are pending.

1

Diary No.25816/2026
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. We do not find any error not to speak of any error of law
in the impugned Judgment passed by the High Court dismissing the
Criminal Appeal preferred by the petitioner.
3. However,  taking into consideration the fact that the
petitioner has been undergoing life imprisonment for more than
13  years, we  permit  the  petitioner-convict  to  prefer  a
representation addressed to the competent authority of the State
Government praying for remission of sentence.  If any such
representation is preferred, the State shall look into the same
at the earliest and decide it in accordance with law, more
particularly, the Remission Policy prevailing on the date of the
commission of the offence or any other policy which is more
beneficial to the petitioner.
4. With the aforesaid,  this Special Leave Petition stands
disposed of.
5. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.

immediately surrender and make a statement in this regard on the
next date of hearing.  If he surrenders, we shall consider
remitting the matter to the High Court for consideration of his
appeal on merits.

SLP(C) No. 7722/2023 etc.
Amicus Curiae 
Mr. Sandeep Sudhakar Deshmukh, Adv.
Mr. Nishant Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Ankur Savadikar, Adv.
                   Mr. Kartik Sharma, Adv. 
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. A.K. De, Adv.
                   Ms. Ananya De, Adv.
                   Mr. Chandrakanta Sahoo, Adv.
                   Mr. Pramit Saxena, AOR                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                                     
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Having heard the learned counsel for the Insurance Company and
the learned Amicus Curiae at length, we find no grounds made out to
interfere with the impugned judgments passed by the High Court.
More so, in the light of Section 64VB of the Insurance Act, 1938,
read with Sections 147(4) and 147(5) of the Motor Vehicles Act,
1988 (as they stood then), and in the light of the decision of this
Court in  United India Insurance Company Limited vs. Laxmamma &
Ors.1.  The special leave petitions are, accordingly, dismissed.
Before parting with the case, we would like to place on record
our appreciation and gratitude for the able and erudite assistance
rendered by Mr. Sandeep Sudhakar Deshmukh, learned Amicus Curiae.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(DEEPAK GUGLANI)                             (PREETI SAXENA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                         COURT MASTER (NSH)

                   Mr. Rameshwar Prasad Goyal, AOR                 
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Abhikalp Pratap Singh, AOR                  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard the learned senior counsel appearing for
the petitioner and the learned counsel appearing for the respondent
Nos.1 to 5.
Despite notice served, none appears for the victim. 
We have also perused the record pertaining to the earlier
proceedings in which the victim did not want to pursue. If she does
not want to pursue the present proceedings also, which is at the
stage of recording the evidence, it will only be a futile exercise.
Prima facie, we are of the view that she does not appear
to be interested in pursuing the matter any further, even by

--- Page 765 ---
2
extending cooperation to the departmental proceedings. 
The learned senior counsel appearing for the petitioner
submits that she has already left the respondent Nos.1 to 5’s
office. 
The learned counsel appearing for the respondent Nos.1 to
5 seeks time to verify whether she will be interested in giving
evidence in the departmental proceedings.
List the matter on 09.09.2026.

DR. BINA MODI                                      Petitioner(s)

THE STATE OF UTTARAKHAND & ORS.                    Respondent(s)

         HON'BLE  MR. JUSTICE PRASANNA B. VARALE
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Sidharth Luthra, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. S. Nagamuthu, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Rajinder Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Karan Sharma, AOR
                   Mr. Rohit Kaliyar, Adv.
                   Mr. Mohit Siwach, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Rajesh Chander Kumar Rohra, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Ritesh Khatri, AOR
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard the learned senior counsel appearing for
the parties. 
Though we are not inclined to interfere with the impugned
order on merits, the fact remains, as indicated in para 34 of the

--- Page 767 ---
2
impugned order, that the police itself is likely to file a final
report and, therefore, there was no need for the High Court to go
into the merits of the case.
In such view of the matter, notwithstanding the impugned
order passed by the High Court, liberty is given to the petitioner
to work out an appropriate remedy in accordance with law, against
the final report which is to be filed. 
The Special Leave Petitions stand disposed of in the
above terms, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.    
(SWETA BALODI)                                  (POONAM VAID)

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No.10213/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  13-05-2026
in MCRC No. 20745/2026 passed by the High Court of Madhya Pradesh
Principal Seat at Jabalpur]
ZAID KHAN                                          Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH                            Respondent(s)
(IA No. 166467/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT, IA No. 168864/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T., IA
No. 166468/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T. AND IA No. 168863/2026
- PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES)
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Anirudh Sharma, AOR
                   Mr. Varun Kesharwani, Adv.
                   Mr. Sundaram, Adv.                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Sridhar Potaraju, A.A.G.
                   Ms. Mrinal Gopal Elker, AOR
                   Ms. Chamundeswari Pemmasani, Adv.
                   Ms. Silpi S Swain, Adv.                   
                   
                   Mr. Akshat Shrivastava, AOR
                   Mrs. Pooja Shrivastava, Adv.
                   Mr. Palash Pareek, Adv.
                   Ms. Ankita Dutta, Adv.                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Having heard the learned counsel appearing for the petitioner
and having gone through the materials on record, we find no good
ground to interfere with the impugned Order passed by the High
Court.
2. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, dismissed.

                             O R D E R
Opportunity to respondent no. 1 to file counter affidavit has
already been declined. 
As per the Office report, learned counsel for the petitioner
has filed affidavit alongwith proof of Dasti stating that the
notice was served to SHO, Sirgitti, Bilaspur, Chhattisgarh. As
notice served in the manner described in the affidavit is not Dasti
i.e., service through party, affidavit alongwith proof of Dasti
cannot be accepted as notice duly served by way of Dasti.

2
Further, service report from the concerned SHO has not been
received despite reminders.
Issue D.O. Letter.

concerned  learned  Advocate-on-Record  for  information  and

ITEM NO.9               COURT NO.15               SECTION IX
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  23667/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  26-02-2026
in COST No. 2187/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Bombay at Nagpur]
SHAILESH ANKUSH DHANVIJAY & ANR.                   Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
PRAVIN MANSARAM WALDE & ORS.                       Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION and I.R.)
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE S.V.N. BHATTI
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE N.V. ANJARIA
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Arun Bhardwaj, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Ashu Tiwari, Adv.
                   Ms. Muskan Jain, Adv.
                   Ms. Khushi Sood, Adv.
                   Ms. Samridhi Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Varun Kapur, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Mr. Arun Bhardwaj, learned senior counsel, draws a line
of distinction in paragraph ‘19’ of the impugned order,
arguing that the order, at best, can be justified only
insofar as it seeks to preserve the property intact
during the pendency of the litigation between the
parties. The agreement, which is unenforceable as of
date, does not, first, provide any arrangement between
the  parties  regarding  possession  of  the  scheduled
1

--- Page 875 ---
property. Accordingly, the latter portion of paragraph
‘19’ directs that the plaintiffs and the defendants
will be in joint possession of the scheduled property
as court receivers. Although a clarification is stated
at  the  end  of  the  same  paragraph  that  physical
possession shall remain with the defendants, it is
argued that, at this stage, while considering a prayer
for  the  grant  or  refusal  of  an  injunction,  the
direction to confer the status of joint possession on
the  plaintiffs  along  with  the  defendants  stands
illegal.
2. Issue  notice  to  the  respondents,  returnable  on
28.09.2026.
3. There shall be a stay of the direction that both
plaintiffs and the defendants are in joint possession
as court receivers until further orders.
(RADHA SHARMA)                                  (NIDHI MATHUR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

--- Page 876 ---
Revised
